summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Final Orders – Where the mother sought sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with her, for the father’s time to be unsupervised and limited to special occasions– Where the father sought sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with him, and for the mother’s time with the child to be supervised indefinitely – Where the parties do not have a good co-parenting relationship and are highly conflictual – Where each of the parties contend the child is at an unacceptable risk of harm in the event the Court made orders as sought by the other party – Where the relationship between the father and the child is not able to be maintained and supported if the child remains living with the mother. FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Family violence – Where the mother alleges the father perpetrated family violence during the relationship – Where the mother alleges the child was exposed to family violence – Where the father denies perpetrating family violence or exposing the child to family violence – Where to accept the mother’s allegation in the face of the father’s denial requires an acceptance of her evidence – Consideration of the absence of any probative cross-examination, the father’s denials, and findings that the mother was an unconvincing and unreliable witness – Where the Court is not satisfied that the mother has discharged the onus of proof. FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Enmeshment – Child’s wishes – Where the Court finds that there is a strong enmeshment between the mother’s views and the child’s stated wishes – Where the Court does not attribute significant weight to the child’s wishes.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

Before the Court are proceedings pursuant to Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) in relation to X born in 2013 (“the child”). The parents separated when the child was approximately two years old. The single expert during her cross-examination described the child in the following way:
She is anxious, she is confused, and she has been traumatised by the experience that she has had for almost her entire childhood as well.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.23 line 30)
The experience to which the single expert was referring is the hostility and conflict created by the parents.
The child identifies as non-binary. To the extent possible, these reasons will refer to the child as ‘the child’ and where appropriate by the pronouns ‘they/them’ except where it otherwise records what is stated in a document or where to do so would distort the meaning or context.
At the commencement of the hearing, the mother broadly sought orders for the child to live with her, sole decision-making responsibility, and for the child to spend supervised time with the father for a minimum of four hours each alternate Sunday until the child turns 14 and from that date in accordance with the child’s wishes. Towards the end of the mother’s cross‑examination when pressed about what orders she thought were in the child’s best interests, she said the child’s safety really meant that there should be no time with the father.
However, by the time of submissions, while she persisted with an order the child live with her and she have sole decision-making responsibility, she sought orders for the child to spend time with the father by agreement and in the absence of agreement for a period of three hours on each of the father’s birthday, the child’s birthday, Father’s Day and Christmas Day. All such time was to occur unsupervised. No evidence was called by the mother to explain the change in her case from supervised time to unsupervised time. The mother’s orders are more precisely set out in Exhibit 110.
The Independent Children’s Lawyer (“the ICL”) for his part broadly sought orders for the mother to have sole decision-making responsibility and for the child to spend unsupervised time with the father on the first Sunday of each month and on Father’s Day between 10.00 am and 5.00 pm. The orders sought by the ICL are more precisely set out in Exhibit 109.
The father for his part sought orders for sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with him, and for the child to spend no time with the mother for a period of six months and thereafter on a supervised basis for a minimum of two hours each fortnight. The father’s orders by the time of submissions were largely the same as those sought by him at the commencement of the hearing. The orders as sought by the father were more precisely set out in Exhibit 111.
The parties’ orders were informed in part from their respective contentions that the proposals of the other parent posed an unacceptable risk of harm to the child.

BACKGROUND

The mother was born in 1966 and is aged 59 years. The father was born in 1971 and was aged 54 years at the time of the hearing.
The parties commenced cohabitation in 2003 and married in 2006. The parties lived in Country QQ, the United States of America, and then Australia from 2009. There was a period of separation between April 2012 and January 2013, with the parties separating on a final basis in 2015 and a Divorce Order issuing in 2016.
There is one child of the relationship aged 12 at the time of the hearing and in Year 6 at school. The child was conceived by IVF while the parties were briefly separated with the father as sperm donor and a third-party donor egg.
The mother contends the father perpetrated family violence during the relationship. She says the pattern of behaviours was not an isolated incident, but part of a broader dynamic of coercive control. Her allegations of family violence were denied by the father.
Between separation and January 2016, the father spent time with the child on an intermittent basis which he says was restricted by the mother. The mother says that during this time she was fearful of the father and was concerned about the emotional impact that it was having on the child. She says that in consultation with the child’s daycare, visits with the father were reduced to once a week in the afternoon and then progressed to twice a week in the afternoon.
Following the father spending time with the child in January 2016, the mother alleged the child reported behaviours and made various disclosures which the mother reported to healthcare professionals and police. She says:
Later that evening, while sitting on their potty, [X] said “Daddy gave me a bath”. I wondered about the necessity of [Mr Kirill] giving [X] a bath, as their clothes were not dirty or stained and their underwear not soiled ([X] was toilet trained by then). [X] leaned forward, opening their mouth wide and tried to kiss me on the lips with an open mouth. Stunned by their actions, I recoiled back and they said “No mummy you do it like this”. [X] opened their mouth wide again and tilting their head slightly and once again leaning in towards my lips.
By that time, [X] had only kissed me of their own accord approximately twice since birth and never opened mouth, just a small kiss on the cheek. Our usual mode of showing affection to one another is to hug and cuddle. [X] had not regularly witnessed any physical affection between [Mr Kirill] and me, nor, as far as I was aware, had they observed romantic affection between other adults, either in person or on television. I was unaware of [Mr Kirill’s] relationship status at the time, but given the brief nature of [X’s] visits with him during the week after daycare or on Saturday mornings, I did not expect that [X] would have seen him kissing anyone during those times.
Concerned about [X’s] disclosure and their actions, I decided to take [X] to the GP early the next morning, [...] January, The GP referred us to the hospital for proper investigation.
I took [X] to [RR Hospital], where [X] was examined by a doctor on the paediatric ward who also discussed their case with the sexual abuse unit at [SS Hospital]. A report was made to FACS by the attending paediatrician. I was advised by the paediatrician to keep [X] in my care, for them not to have visits with their father and if [X] did anything else concerning to ask them “Who showed you how to do that?” or “Where did you learn that?” without identifying or asking about any other person.
The following evening, [...] January, I gave [X] their first bath after their Friday visit with [Mr Kirill]. When [X] sat down in the bath the first thing they did was open their legs and with two fingers, one on each side of their vulva, open their vagina up wide and then with one finger start rubbing their clitoris. I asked [X] “Where did you learn that?” [X] stopped, closed their legs and then said I need some water.
I gave [X] one of their water play toy scoops and then they spread their legs and scooped some water into the cup and poured it directly over their clitoris. I said, “what are you doing honey?” [X] then said, “I need to fill my vagina up with water” and [X] then stuck their finger into their vagina. I said, “bub where did you learn that?”, following the advice given to me by the paediatrician the previous day. [X] did not respond and was quiet.
...
On 15 May 2016, [X] was in the bath playing with bath toys. [X] looked up at me and said, “Can I tell you about daddy touching my vagina?”. I responded, “Of course you can, you can tell mummy anything”. [X] then went quiet and hung their head. I did not want to push the conversation any further, so I took [X] out of the bath, gave them a cuddle and put them to bed.
In early 2016, a notification was made to the Family and Community Services (as it was then known) hotline by the mother. The mother also contacted police to report the child’s statements. The child is not seen or spoken to by police on this occasion and the mother says that she was told to keep the child in her care and not send them to daycare for at least a week.
On 1 February 2016, the father commenced proceedings in the Family Court of Australia (as it was then known). On the same day, the mother’s solicitors sent a letter to the father’s solicitors to the effect that his time with the child needed to be suspended due to disclosures of concern and pending investigations.
In mid-February 2016, the child was interviewed by the Joint Investigation Response Team (“JIRT”). The father was also interviewed by JIRT. In March 2016, the father was informed that “sexual harm is not substantiated and there will be no on-going casework/investigations from [Suburb KK] JIRT” (Exhibit 9). The letter says:
The investigation is over and I have assessed that based on the available information/evidence I cannot confirm that [X] was sexually abused.

To my knowledge there have been no disclosures of sexual assault from [X].

(Exhibit 9)
The letter also says, in terms of supervised time, the following:
I may have suggested supervised contact to [Ms Tevye] and you, ONLY because this was the only option available that would allow contact between [X] and yourself, given [Ms Tevye] would not allow contact without it being supervised. I saw this as an interim measure until the matter could be resolved through the family court. I only suggested this so that [X] could see you, not because there are on-going concerns from [Suburb KK] JIRT.

(Exhibit 9)
Interim orders were made on 20 April 2016 which provided for the father to spend supervised time with the child.
On 27 February 2017, Dr UU, the single expert in the 2016 proceedings, released her report which included recommendations that no further supervised visits were necessary. The report said:
Although [Ms Tevye] continued to believe JIRT referred to a “moderate risk” of abuse after their assessment, the senior child protection caseworker at FaCS wrote to [Mr Kirill] in [March 2016] that supervised contact had been suggested “as an interim measure until the matter could be resolved through the family court... so that [X] could see you, not because there are on-going concerns from [Suburb KK] JIRT” (email dated [...] March 2016).

It is possible to hypothesis that [X] had become shamed by her mother’s negative focus on her self-stimulation ([in early 2016], after [Ms Tevye] question her about her masturbation, she “hung her head”), her two medical reviews, JIRT questioning and then finally a DVD (which she was enjoying) raised the issue of “touches”.

...

On balance, I cannot conclude it is likely a sexual act of abuse has been perpetrated on [X] but clearly this remains an issue for the Court to determine. The questions remain whether supervised contact is more detrimental than no contact and is there an unacceptable risk if contact is not supervised.

...

To protect both [X] and [Mr Kirill] from further allegations he is a risk of harm to his daughter, it would be a wise precaution by [Mr Kirill] to spend time with [X] while [Ms VV] is nearby (ie not formally supervising them) until [X] is around eight years old.

(Exhibit 4)
On 1 March 2018, the 2016 proceedings were finalised by the parties attending mediation resulting in a settlement and the making of final parenting and property orders which provided for the child to live with the mother and for the father’s time with the child to progress to five nights per fortnight by 2020 as well as school holiday time. The orders provide for the child to receive an assessment as to a possible sensory processing disorder.
The mother’s affidavit contends that thereafter there was an ongoing pattern of aggressive, hostile and threatening behaviour by the father involving threats of contravention proceedings and the filing of such applications involving matters such as the appropriateness of changeover location, FaceTime calls that allegedly did not go through, the mother failing to provide her address to the father, a change in childcare arrangements for the child and delayed commencement of overnight time.
In July 2018, the child started attending therapy with Ms M. The child attended sessions with Ms M until January 2020 on a fortnightly and then monthly basis. The mother says that these sessions ceased when the child told the mother they were too frightened to speak to Ms M, as the father had told the child he would find out what they were saying in therapy.
In January 2019, a contravention application was threatened by the father about a cruise consent form that had not been provided in original form from the mother to the father. Further conflict between the parties arose during the COVID-19 period when the father raises issues about whose care the child should be in on a school day.
Beginning in 2021, the mother contended that the father made comments about the child’s physical appearance amounting to ‘body-shaming’ which caused the child distress and reportedly restricted the child’s food intake. The father denied such behaviour and says that the child’s reports to the mother are inconsistent with the relationship he then shared with the child.
In January 2024, the mother contended that after the child spends one week with the father, the child returned to the mother’s care with numerous sores on their legs, arms, chest and face. She says that there are numerous subsequent reports from the child that they are compulsively picking at their skin when stressed. She says:
Following an overnight stay with [Mr Kirill], on Wednesday 7 February 2024, [X] returned from school and said “My dad saw me picking my legs. and he started yelling at me, saying ‘Stop, why are you doing that?’ in a loud angry voice.” [X] expressed that this reaction increased their stress levels, saying, “Mum, I am picking because I am stressed and that made me even more stressed.”
[X] further described that later in the evening, while sitting on the couch, [X] was unconsciously picking at their skin when [Mr Kirill], seated next to [X], slapped [X’s] leg hard enough that it hurt while again shouting “Stop!” [X] said that they felt scared and moved away from him. Later that night, [X] was still upset and asked me, “Why did my dad hit me?”.
On 9 February 2024, [X] made a further disclosure regarding a prior incident involving physical harm. [X] said to me: “After my dad hit me, I went to school and asked my friends if they thought it was okay for a parent to hit a kid because I felt upset about it and they said no way. I knew what my dad had done was bad and my friends thought so too.”
On 11 February 2024, following a short FaceTime call with [Mr Kirill], [X] approached me visibly distressed and showed me a bleeding mark on their leg. I asked how [X] had been feeling when it happened. [X] explained that during the call, [Mr Kirill] asked them, “Are you still picking?” [X] reported feeling hurt and mocked when he continued, stating that he had a “solution” and would “wrap [X] up like a mummy” to prevent the behaviour, adding, “Mum, it’s not funny, he was making fun of me and I didn’t like that.”
On 15 February 2024 at 7.34pm [X] called me from the bathroom, they just had a shower before bed. [X] said, “Mum I just picked at my arm and it is bleeding now”. I replied “That’s ok hon, do you want a bandaid on it?” [X] went on to say “I was thinking about how my dad hit me and I was thinking, what if he does it again?” I asked “Would you like to talk about how you are feeling?” To which [X] said “I’m feeling worried it’s going to happen again this weekend”.
On 19 February 2024, I picked up [X] from school following their weekend with [Mr Kirill]. While driving home, [X] disclosed an incident that occurred that morning during the car ride to school. [X] said to me: “Mum, this morning I was sitting in the front seat of the car next to my dad on the way to school, and I guess I was picking at my leg. He grabbed my hand tight and first made my hand hit my leg and said, ‘Pick, pick, pick,’ and then he used my hand to slap my face a few times and said, ‘Hit, hit, hit.’ And it hurt and frightened me.”
[X] appeared visibly distressed when recounting this. I was concerned about [X’s] emotional response and the seriousness of the physical interaction described.
In June 2024, the child commenced sessions with Ms WW through Victims Services without the father’s knowledge or consent. In August 2024, Ms WW provisionally diagnosed the child with an adjustment disorder, acute anxiety with psychotic features and extreme stress. These sessions ceased in December 2024. The father says of Ms WW:
(c) I have now seen the Certificate provided by [Ms WW] in relation to the consultation she apparently had with [X] where [X] has allegedly disclosed physical assault, verbal abuse, gaslighting, parental alienation that I have done to her . I deny all of these assertions. I have never hit [X]; I have never verbally abused [X]. I have never engaged in gaslighting, parental alienation, stonewalling or neglect. I believe these are the words of the Applicant, who has been able to find a therapist to support her assertions.
On 14 July 2024, the mother reported to the father the child had locked themself in their room and would not be attending changeover for the start of the school holiday period. The mother contended that she held concerns for the child’s safety and says that as the father refused to participate in discussions regarding appropriate sleeping arrangements for the child, she withheld the child.
On 15 July 2024, the mother sent a letter to the father stating that the father’s time with the child would cease because of a decline in the child’s mental health. The letter lists numerous health professionals the mother says advised her to cease time. At no time prior to sending the letter did the mother raise any of the stated concerns with the father. He says:
During all the time that [X] has spent in my care since she was 5 years of age, she has never raised any concerns, never shown any fear, never told me that she felt uncomfortable with me and never once requested to go back to the Applicant.
On 13 August 2024, the mother commenced these proceedings.
At an interim hearing on 11 September 2024, it was agreed by consent that the mother, the father, and the child would attend family therapy with the mother to nominate three family therapists. The father selected Ms R at Suburb YY. The father says:
On [...] October 2024, I attended a third session with [Ms R]. During this session, [Ms R] informed me that:
(a) “I have now seen [Ms Tevye] for one session. I have to tell you that I am not getting much traction with [Ms Tevye] and she does not want to engage in family therapy. I am a bit confused about why [Ms Tevye] has agreed to Family Therapy because she is not bothering or wanting to attend sessions”

(b) “[Mr Kirill]. Can you clarify whether I am expected to work on family reconciliation or to just write an assessment for the Court? I love working with families and I am hoping to help your family and work through your issues to help you move forward” l responded “Well, the orders are clear that we are to engage in family therapy. You only write a letter to the Court if there is no attendance or you form the view that you can’t assist us”.

On 4 November 2024, the mother received an email from Ms R stating that the next step in the process proposed by the Court would involve an appointment where the father, mother, and the child would attend but not necessarily together. The mother says that when she advised the child that Ms R want to see her again, the child became extremely distressed, was crying and was screaming. The mother says:
It took me an hour to calm [X], after which [X] presented as flat and withdrawn, and stated that they wanted to hurt themself again. I remained with [X] until they fell asleep to ensure their safety. The following morning, [X] remained in a distressed state. While I briefly left the room to use the bathroom, [X] self-harmed, making four gash marks on their ankle. Concerned about [X’s] ability to cope at school, I contacted their teacher to ask for support. As the teacher was absent that day, [X] was instead seen by the school counsellor, [Ms PP], who conducted a suicide risk assessment and developed coping strategies with [X]. I was infom1ed that a report was made, given that self-harming had occurred on school grounds.
During the school day, I received an email from [X] via their school account that read, “I did some on my arm in the school bathroom. I’m really sorry.’’
When I collected [X] from school, I observed additional gash marks on their arm and near their wrist. [X] said, “Mum I am so sorry, I tried not to do it but when I went to the toilet I thought about seeing him again and l couldn’t stop.
...

89l. On 11 November 2024, I received an email from [Ms R] stating that she had decided not to proceed with a family therapy session. [Ms R] instead requested to see [X] alone on [...] November 2024 to further explore their relationship and experiences with [Mr Kirill]. [X] attended that session, and ceased self-harming after [X] was reassured that they would not be required to attend a session with [Mr Kirill] present.
The father says that he believes that the mother simply agreed to attend family therapy as a ruse to further delay the process and his ability to spend any time with the child. He says:
On [...] November 2024, I attended a fifth session with my fiancée [Ms ZZ] with [Ms R]. During this session, [Ms R] told me:
(a) “I saw [X] a second time, on Thursday last but it was like meeting a different child than the one I initially saw. [X] was very different, much more reserved and using words that an eleven-year-old just would not use. After I finished the session with [X], I emailed [Ms Tevye] to see if she had said anything to [X] in between sessions, because it seems to me that [X] had been coached. [Ms Tevye] has now accused me of not listening properly to [X] during our first session”.
(As per the original)
Ms R, in the letter attached to her affidavit, said the following:
I have consulted with [Mr Kirill] (at 5 x consultations), [Ms Tevye] (on 1 x 90 minute consultation) and [X] (on 2 x 60 minute consultations). I have read much of the previous file notes and court filings and consulted with previous mental health professionals who have been consulted by this family currently and in past years.

I have formed the view that [Mr Kirill] is fully motivated to find a resolution to the estrangement from his daughter, [X], and is keen to resolve any misunderstandings that may exist. I have formed the view that he is unable to make sense of [X’s] seemingly sudden refusal to continue her access times with him.

I have formed the view that [Ms Tevye] is not interested or motivated to attend family therapy sessions nor to encourage the relationship between the daughter, [X], and her father.

There is no evidence to suggest that [X] is willing to engage in a family therapy session with her father nor to resolve any unresolved issues with her father. It is unclear how much her view is influenced by her individual thought and experiences or how much it is influenced by her mother’s wishes, feelings and anxieties or by others. Some of her opinions and expressions are unusually sophisticated for an 11 year old. I have not formed a strong opinion regarding this observation but it is of concern if [X] is being influenced by others opinions.

Rather than disengaging from the therapeutic process, I have formed the view that there was not a willingness to engage at the outset. Consequently, no family therapy sessions have been attempted.
On 6 December 2024, interim parenting orders discharged the 2018 final parenting orders. Orders are also made ending the child’s therapy with Ms WW and for the child to attend upon a clinical psychologist for the ongoing management of their mental health, with the orders providing a process by which the parties were to agree upon such psychologist.
The child is then engaged with another treater, Ms XX. The mother says:
[In] December 2024, I had an appointment with [X’s] GP [Dr AB] to obtain recommendations from the GP for a clinical psychologist. He provided me with a list of names and different psychology practices from which I made initial enquires regarding availability - those names being [Ms AC], [Ms AD] and [Ms XX].
[In] January 2025 I had a parent only session with [Ms XX] as she wanted some initial information, I informed [Ms XX] that [Mr Kirill] was aware of [X] seeing her as he had been notified by his lawyer, his contact information was on file with the practice and [Ms XX] should speak with him if he reaches out and makes an appointment.
On [...] January 2025 [X] had an initial session with [Ms XX] alone. After the session [X] said “[Ms XX] asked me if it was ok if she spoke with my dad if he contacts her and I said yes but I don’t to speak with him.”
...
[In] February 2025, I was informed by the [AE Mental Health Services] manager that [X’s] sessions with [Ms XX] would have to cease, because [Mr Kirill] had contacted the clinic and stated that he did not consent to [X] being treated by [Ms XX], on the basis that “he wasn’t informed [X] was seeing [Ms XX]”, notwithstanding the correspondence sent to [Mr Kirill’s] solicitor on 14 and 23 January 2025.
The practice manager, [Ms AF], advised that [Ms XX] would call [Mr Kirill] and try to convince him of the need for [X] to continue to have psychological support.
On 18 February 2025 the practice manager called me again to say that [Ms XX] had been unsuccessful and [Mr Kirill] was still refusing to consent. [Ms XX] would give [X] a call to let [X] know so I left it to [Ms XX] to explain the situation.
On 21 February 2025 [Ms XX] called [X] to let [X] know that she could no longer see them due to [Mr Kirill] not consenting. [Ms XX] had notified [Mr Kirill] that she would be making this call. After [X] spoke with [Ms XX], [X] was upset and said “My dad isn’t letting me talk to anyone because he doesn’t want me to rat him out”.
The father says that he holds concerns as to the number of clinicians the child has seen and their competence. He says:
Since 2024, possibly late 2023, the Applicant has again taken [X] to see numerous health professionals without my knowledge or consent, including:
[Ms U], Provisional Psychologist, [AT Mental Health Services]
[Ms WW], Psychotherapist, Family and Child Consultant and Victims Services provider, [Suburb AG] - 19 sessions 2024
[Ms AH] from CYMHS (Child and Youth Mental Health Service), [Suburb AJ]
[Ms AK], Child Support, Women and Children First, Community Domestic Violence service, [Suburb AL]
[Ms AM], Domestic Violence Counsellor, Lifeline [Region AN] (2024)
[Ms AO], Trauma Therapist, registered clinical counsellor, [Suburb AJ] (2024)
[Ms AP], Trauma Therapist with Victims Services, [Suburb AJ] (2024)
[Ms AQ], the Early Response Service Coordinator from [EE Health Service] (2024)
[Ms AR] – [AV Family Services] [Suburb AJ], Counsellor/ Art Therapist, [Suburb AJ]
[Dr AB] - GP, [AW Health Services], saw [X] for concerns ringing in the ears.
[Ms AS], Senior Clinical Psychologist, [Region AN] Child and Youth.
[Ms XX] - Clinical Psychologist, [AE Mental Health Services], [Suburb YY] (4 sessions, Jan - February 2025).
248. [X] has also been interviewed again by the Police and DCJ in July 2024.
In accordance with the interim parenting orders made 6 December 2024, time with the father was to be supervised by a professional supervision service. Commencing March 2025, the child refuses to get into the supervisor’s car to go to a supervised visit. In May 2025, the child enters the supervisor’s car but is too distressed to be driven to the location of the visit.
From June 2025, the child attends the supervised visits and engages with the father through head gestures, answering direct questions with brief answers, and occasional smiles and laughs.
The child does not attend supervised time for the day immediately preceding the commencement of the hearing and the weekend in the middle of the hearing.

APPLICABLE LAW

Parenting proceedings are governed by Pt VII of the Act. In making a parenting order, s 60CA of the Act requires the Court to regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration. In determining what is in the child’s best interests, s 60CC(1) of the Act provides the Court must consider the matters set out in s 60CC(2) of the Act. The matters to be considered are as follows:
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;
(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

In addressing the matters in s 60CC(2A) of the Act, the Court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child together with any family violence order that applies in relation to the child or a member of the child’s family.
Section 60CG of the Act requires the Court, when making a parenting order, to ensure the order does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence and is consistent with an existing family violence order.
The mother seeks a factual finding that the father perpetrated family violence. Family violence in light of the statutory considerations is a matter relevant to the making of a parenting order. Each of the parties contend the child is at an unacceptable risk of harm in the event the Court made orders as sought by the other party.
The answer to a question of future risk can be informed by anterior factual findings including findings of family violence. In that respect, the Full Court in Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 observed as follows:
Long ago, in parenting proceedings characterised by one parent’s allegation that the other had sexually abused their child, the High Court of Australia (“the High Court”) emphasised the distinction between two very different things: on the one hand, proving alleged sexual abuse according to the civil standard of proof and, on the other, establishing the risk of the feared sexual abuse occurring in the future: (M v M (1988) 166 CLR 69).
In respect of the first issue, the High Court said (at 76):
In considering an allegation of sexual abuse, the court should not make a positive finding that the allegation is true unless the court is so satisfied according to the civil standard of proof, with due regard to the factors mentioned in Briginshaw v. Briginshaw.

(Footnote omitted)
...
In relation to the second question pertaining to risk, the High Court said (at 77–78):
... [T]he court must determine whether on the evidence there is a risk of sexual abuse occurring if custody or access be granted and assess the magnitude of that risk. ... [T]he test is best expressed by saying that a court will not grant custody or access to a parent if that custody or access would expose the child to an unacceptable risk of sexual abuse.
...
M v M put beyond doubt the proposition that courts exercising jurisdiction under Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) must protect children from credible risks of harm due to sexual abuse. Such risks, like all prospective events, are capable of classification in only one of three mutually exclusive categories: possibilities, probabilities, or certainties. Once it is accepted courts should (and do) react to dangers in the form of risks of harm which may merely be possibilities, it is an oxymoron to expect such possibilities to then be forensically proven on the balance of probabilities according to the civil standard of proof. By definition, possibilities are not, and could never be, probabilities. Risks of harm are not susceptible of scientific demonstration or proof (CDJ v VAJ (1998) 197 CLR 172 at [151]), but are instead postulated from known historical facts and present circumstances.
In relation to the question of unacceptable risk, Tree J succinctly summarised in Chiles & Petrenko [2024] FedCFamC1A 112; (2024) FLC 94-197 the risk assessment in the following terms:
...It bears repeating that the usual course in undertaking the assessment is to identify the contended risk event, gauge the likelihood of it occurring, and then consider the magnitude of harm which might ensue if the risk event does occur. One then considers whether in the light of those matters the risk of harm is acceptable or not.
The purpose of ameliorative conditions is to mitigate a risk of harm from unacceptable to acceptable, such that when the risk analysis is repeated with the ameliorative condition in place, the relevant risk is assessed as acceptable.
The issue of risk and whether it is unacceptable will be addressed below.

EVIDENCE OF THE SINGLE EXPERT

The single expert prepared a report dated 28 April 2025 which became Exhibit 80 (“the Report”). For the purposes of her cross-examination, on the second-last day of the hearing the single expert read further documents provided by the parties and spoke with the child’s therapist, Ms M.
There was no challenge to the expertise or experience of the single expert. I found her report and oral evidence insightful, logical, and well-reasoned. I accept her evidence.
The single expert recorded the mother raising multiple concerns for the child in the care of the father including the child’s expressed fear and resistance in speaking to the father or seeing him again. The single expert reported the mother felt she had no choice but to act given the child was expressing suicidal ideation and self-harming. The single expert recorded the mother believed the father had not acted in the child’s best interests and did not support their therapy engagement. She denied she had acted unilaterally. She reported to the single expert her belief that the cause of the child’s suicidal ideation and self-harming behaviour was “exposure to family violence by her father, his coercive controlling behaviour, being given the silent treatment, body shaming, being put down, and being with a parent who has emotion dysregulation, have all contributed” (Report, paragraph 24).
The single expert recorded the father describing the child as having “two personalities” (Report, paragraph 50). He reported that the child “has a dual life to cope with her surroundings and is aware of the tension between her parents” and “that away from the conflict she thrives in her environment (Report, paragraph 50). He said that all that changed a year ago when she started to engage in “self-picking and scratching” (Report, paragraph 50).
The single expert recorded:
[Mr Kirill] denies sexually abusing, physically abusing, or threatening to harm [X]. He denies that he is a risk of harm to [X]. He believes that [Ms Tevye] is a risk of harm to [X] and he is concerned about [X] in her mother’s care such that the litigation is ongoingly focused on him and that [X] is a collateral damage in this conflict.
In relation to the mother’s allegations against him, the single expert recorded the father in the following terms:
[Mr Kirill] denies current symptoms consistent with a pervasive mood disorder. He denies any history of perceptual disturbances. He has a history of panic attacks which he experienced in the context of him leaving his employment in bad circumstances (this occurred before [X] was born). He does not describe himself as a generally anxious person, however, [Ms Tevye] reportedly puts him through a lot of anxiety and he is vigilant as to what she may do next. He denies he has a problem with his anger; in fact, he feels that he is the opposite, particularly when he has been accused of using the silent treatment, with [Mr Kirill] questioning that he cannot be angry and silent at the same time. He agrees that he used silence as a defence mechanism as a young child, which he learnt from his mother as to how to manage conflict. He said that he has learnt that this strategy is not acceptable and he needs to communicate and accept that all emotions are acceptable. He said that he has never given [X] the silent treatment. When asked how he is coping, he believes he is managing well although experiences some stress and anxiety; he has the support of [Ms ZZ], colleagues, [cultural] groups, his psychologist and long term friends.
The father’s partner was interviewed by the single expert. The single expert recorded:

She denies that she has seen [Mr Kirill] angry or raise his voice. She denies that she was coerced or told what to say in her interview or that she has seen any Court affidavit. ...

Under a heading, “Terms of Reference” the single expert opined as follows:
This matter is characterised by longstanding litigation, high parental conflict, and significantly entrenched opposing views held by each parent. When first assessed by [Dr UU] in December 2016, [X] presented as a well-adjusted toddler with a good relationship with both parents, emotionally regulated, and as able to transition between her parents. She is now a highly anxious and traumatised pre-adolescent who has struggled to transition between her parents and is rejecting her relationship with her father. I hold significant concerns regarding [X’s] mental health, and her prognosis for future wellbeing is poor if the current circumstances persist. [X] has been exposed to this conflict for almost her entire life, both prior to and following the separation, and it has had a clear and cumulative impact on her psychological wellbeing.
Under the heading, “Matters set out in s 60CC, 61DA and 65DAAA” the single expert said as follows:
[Ms Tevye] refers to herself as being the victim of coercive control, which [Mr Kirill] denies and counter alleges she was, and is controlling. It is apparent that the parental relationship was marked with unhappiness, conflict, and stress, particularly in the context of the long term infertility. [Mr Kirill] agrees that he withdrew in the relationship, which appears to support [Ms Tevye’s] allegation about the silent treatment she endured. If [Mr Kirill] engaged in silent treatment behaviour during the marriage in a persistent manner, this would likely have contributed to significant emotional disconnection within the relationship and could be experienced as dismissive. If repeated or used as a form of punishment, it can be psychologically harmful, distressing and confusing, particularly if used to withdraw affection and this may reflect an unhealthy communication pattern within [Mr Kirill]. I am unable to substantiate either way either parents’ narrative or whether [X] has been exposed to [Mr Kirill]’s behaviour as [Ms Tevye] alleges, however at present, there is no independent evidence substantiating the alleged behaviour as harmful or abusive toward [X] and professionals involved with the family, including from those with a systemic perspective, have noted that [X’s] reaction to her father appears disproportionate to any reported events. It is noted that [Mr Kirill]’s former partner, who was interviewed by [Dr UU] in 2016, and at the time was supportive of [Mr Kirill], has now written an affidavit supporting [Ms Tevye’s] account of him alleging similar behave in their relationship and her being coerced by [Mr Kirill] to support him in 2016. It is difficult to give credence as to which account of hers is accurate. It is also noted that the DCJ assessment has not culminated in a substantiation of any allegations.
[Mr Kirill] presented as calm, cooperative, and at times despondent during the assessment. He expressed a desire to restore his relationship with his daughter and conveyed frustration and being upset at the current level of estrangement. He denied the allegations made against him by [Ms Tevye] and believes that she has led a concerted effort to remove him from [X’s] life. If the father’s account is to be accepted, then [X’s] rejection of her father may not be based on substantiated harm but rather influenced by enmeshment with her mother, loyalty binds, and her father’s lack of attunement. This view would be consistent with [X’s] emotional response toward her father as have been noted by professionals to be disproportionate to the reported events, raising concerns about the influence of [X’s] expressed views.
[Ms Tevye] presented as caring and attentive and fiercely protective and concerned about [X’s] safety in her father’s care. If [Ms Tevye’s] account is to be adopted, then she and [X] have been exposed to ongoing psychological torment by [Mr Kirill] that has left them both feeling fearful of him.
Given [X’s] mental health and the protracted nature of the parental dispute, timely intervention is critical with regards to appropriate psychological support by an experienced child psychologist who has the capacity to work with [X] systemically without excluding either parent. The school counsellor should be involved with [X], and it lacks child focus that [Mr Kirill] has made the decision to not give consent. Nevertheless, the school counsellor has a duty of care to respond to [X] if she were to need care at school. [Ms M] and [Ms PP] impressed as giving a balanced and child focussed perspective on [X] and both presented as competent to work with this family. Alternatively, an experienced child clinical psychologist can be appointed to see [X]. It is unfair on [X] that she has had many changes in therapists as the therapeutic relationship takes time to build and to have this abruptly repeatedly ceased is not in her best interest.
Neither parent presented with any diagnosable mental health disorder, however they both experience stress and anxiety in the context of ongoing litigation. They should continue to engage with a therapist to support them through the litigation process and subsequently with parenting matters/orders made by the Court.
Continued exposure to chronic hostility or the loss of a meaningful relationship with her biological parent, can cause [X] long term distress, identity confusion, and interpersonal difficulties as [X’s] internal model of relationships is forged on her exposure to the parental relationship and her own relationship with her parents. [X] has a relationship with her egg donor but no ongoing relationship with her biological father. If [Mr Kirill]’s use of the silent treatment toward [X] is ongoing, manipulative, or causing emotional distress, it would warrant further intervention through parenting support and therapy, to ensure [X] feels emotionally safe, heard, and supported. This would have been ideal for the family therapy process that was Court ordered but it is understood that this was unsuccessful. In the absence of substantiated harm to [X] - particularly where professionals have observed that [X’s] rejection of her father may be disproportionate or influenced by loyalty conflicts – should not in itself preclude [X] from having a relationship with her father, particularly if [Mr Kirill] has engaged in therapy and has worked to change those patterns of communication.
Under the heading, “The impact upon the child and her relationship with the mother if the court makes orders as sought by the father” she opined as follows:
Placing [X] in the primary care of her father would mean a significant change in her current circumstances and would need to be managed with sensitivity and professional support. There would need to be confidence that [Mr Kirill] will see the importance of this and be supporting of [X] receiving support and that he would be responsive to therapeutic feedback about his conduct. He has expressed that [X] needs support however he has been concerned about the number of clinicians [X] has seen and their competence.
Given [X’s] current alignment with her mother and rejection of her father, such a transition would likely be met with resistance and distress in the short term. However, if her rejection of her father is more reflective of a coping mechanism rather than a risk of harm, [X] may benefit in the long term from being removed from the enmeshed environment that could be impacting her wellbeing as this is psychologically harmful to her. Whilst this transition may initially cause distress, it may offer [X] a more balanced home environment particularly if the current primary environment is reinforcing dependency, anxiety, and rejection of her father without evidence of harm. This option will need to be considered in light of [Mr Kirill]’s capacity to work closely with a psychologist and to be an emotionally competent parent, as in this instance, [X] has a sensitive and anxious temperament, and caution would need to be had as there is a risk that [X’s] mental health could deteriorate further.
If [X] were to be placed in the primary care of her father, this would be distressing for [X]. The impact on her relationship with her mother would be significant and how [Ms Tevye] responds to the change in primary care would be important, as there would be concern that [Ms Tevye] may undermine the transition and [X] may experience increased distress and difficulty in adjusting. If [Ms Tevye] were to undermine the transition, then a significant period of supervised time with [Ms Tevye] (such as 12 months) would be required to ensure [Ms Tevye] does not continue to undermine the transition. If [Ms Tevye] were not able to commit to supervised time without undermining [Mr Kirill], then a period of no contact with [Ms Tevye] and [X] may be necessary to allow [X] to settle in her father’s care, before supervised time recommences, with a progression to unsupervised time when the Court is satisfied that [Ms Tevye] is compliant with orders.
In relation to the impact upon the child if the Court made orders as sought by the mother, the single expert opined as follows:
If [X] were to have no contact, or only minimal written communication with her father, the long-term impact on her relationship with her father is likely to be significant. While this option would appear to reduce [X’s] immediate distress and exposure to overt conflict, it risks reinforcing [X’s] current beliefs, particularly if those beliefs have developed in the context of loyalty binds or enmeshment and in the absence of substantiated risk. In this scenario, no contact in the absence of substantiated risk may cause long term harm to [X’s] psychological wellbeing, her identity formation, and understanding of her family relationships, and risks permanently severing her relationship with her father and her paternal family
In the absence of any meaningful relationship or ongoing contact, [X] is likely to grow up believing that her father is abusive or harmful and without the opportunity to test or challenge that narrative, which can further entrench a distorted view of her family history.
Ultimately, it appears that [Ms Tevye] does not support [X’s] meaningful relationship with her father and could not outline any significant role that her father should or could have in the future. There is also a concern that it appears that the parental relationship was over before [X’s] conception and that [Ms Tevye] wanted to have a child and saw [Mr Kirill] as a means to achieve her goal. This matter is further complicated by the fact that [X] has an existing relationship with her egg donor, but no relationship with her biological father, whereby there is a situation where her maternal biological origins are known and supported yet her paternal biological heritage is denied or negatively framed.
The single expert was cross-examined by all counsel. Prior to giving her evidence, the single expert spoke with Ms M, the agreed therapist for the child. Ms M told the single expert that the mother had been compliant with getting the child to the therapy sessions, however, she did so through ‘gritted teeth’. Ms M reported that the child was doing well with their mental health and was not engaged in self-harming behaviour but there was no deep engagement in therapy. Ms M reported that the child consistently reported they did not want to have a relationship with the father.
Later in her cross-examination, the single expert agreed that the observations that the mother was bringing the child to therapy through ‘gritted teeth’ was unsurprising and that it was apparent that the mother did not support the therapy.
In response to questions from counsel for the ICL, the single expert confirmed that the child’s mental health had been impacted by the conflict and was exhibiting generalised anxiety. She opined that where a family is in significant conflict it is often difficult for a child to cope with transitions so the way to manage that is to split off from one parent. She opined it was difficult for the child to have a free relationship with both parents which has been the case since X was a young child, and it was no surprise that the child’s current functioning was as it is.
The single expert said the child’s views had been influenced by the mother and that both Ms M and Ms U noted the child had a relationship that was not independent and said that it would be difficult for the child to express an alternate view or consider alternate perspectives of the father, different to those of the mother.
In relation to a change of residence, she said that it would be very difficult and distressing for the child to be separated from the mother. She said that transition to high school was a very difficult time for children and that it is possible that a transition at that time may lead to a deterioration in the child’s mental health and that any change if it was to occur, should occur before high school started but not at the same time.
In relation to the father, she said that he was not obstructive in relation to engagement with therapy and that he has a reflective capacity. She expressed a concern that there was a risk that the child may self-place, given the child is strong-willed, but it may be the case that after a period of adjustment the child may settle in the father’s care. The single expert was cross-examined by the ICL as to the period of time in which the child should have no contact with the mother and was of the view that six months was too long a period of time and that one of the risks on the father’s proposal was that the child would go back to the mother’s household where there was no support for the relationship.
The single expert observed from her reading of the contact notes, there was a plan between the mother and the child. She says that the mother does not want the child to have a relationship with the father and the child is very much aware of that. The single expert said the consequence of the mother’s approach is the child will grow up to:
...believe that her father is this person that is abusive and is not a good person to be around and so in that context I think it will ... have an impact on her ability to form relationships in the future and her ability to consider alternate perspectives and to reality-test relationships moving forward, I think, will be really important risk for her. She will then have trouble with relationships and managing them and having positive relationships with others.
The single expert reiterated that she did not see how the child could go between both households and have a relationship with the father while remaining living with the mother.
The single expert agreed one possibility may be for a form of identity contact if the child remained living in the mother’s care. She said that was not a meaningful relationship but will provide the possibility for the child in the future to be able to contact their father.
In answer to questions from the ICL’s counsel, the expert opined that if there was to be a change to the father’s care, it would be important for the father to be at home during the initial period to ensure the child attended school, comes home and stay in the home environment. There is a risk the child will run away, and the father would need to maintain a steady, sensitive, empathic approach and have some routine so that the child continues to go to school. He would need to be a stable figure.
The single expert said that the father’s reaction to the child’s skin picking was not sensitive, and he could have managed it differently, but the child’s response had been disproportionate.
In answer to questions from the counsel for the mother, she observed that Dr UU had opined that the father had a reflective capacity and that he had demonstrated that reflective capacity with the single expert but had not always done so. She agreed that a risk for the child in the father’s care was not having the capacity to talk to someone about “big emotions”.
In relation to skin picking, the single expert described it as a form of managing the child’s distress and a coping mechanism. In relation to the question of suicidal ideation, she did not believe the child has ever had any plans or intent to do so but has had some passive suicidal thoughts. She agreed that a possible risk in placing the child with the father was a return to skin picking and suicidal ideation, she agreed that there was a significant risk of a deterioration in the child’s mental health and also a risk in circumstances where the child was not engaging with the father and his partner and therefore they may not recognise any significant change. In her cross-examination, she was asked questions about the father’s conduct in removing his consent to the child’s engagement with Ms XX and the school counsellor and agreed that his conduct in doing so was not child-focussed and that it would have been beneficial for the father to have taken a step back and pause for a moment and consider how he could manage the situation without withdrawing his consent.
In cross-examination by counsel for the father, the single expert agreed that Ms WW was not qualified to see the child. She agreed that if the mother was engaging with treaters for the child on the basis they had been sexually assaulted by the father and on the basis that they had domestic violence trauma directly from the father, that it would not be supportive of a relationship with the father and nor would it be a reflection of the child’s reality at the times the child saw Dr UU to which she answered, “Yes” (Transcript 24 November 2025, p.31 line 44). She also agreed that continued therapy for the child that they were unsafe in the father’s care would send that message to the child.
In relation to the mother’s conduct in taking the child to various treaters with the narrative that the child was unsafe in the father’s care and at risk of harm, the single expert said:
My concern would be that the mother would continue to take [X] to different therapists, or shop for therapists, that support her view, and do not engage in any reality testing with the mother or with the child. Also, it also imposes this sense of fear in [X], and anxiety, that she is in danger, and that could generalise to a world view for her, that the world is a scary place, that men are scary, for example, and that is detrimental for a child who is already experiencing some difficulties with her mental health already.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.38 lines 40–46)
In relation to short-term versus long-term risks, the single expert answered questions posed by counsel for the father in the following way:
...I am just going to frame it this way, and you can agree or disagree with me. But in some ways, what his Honour has got to decide is these short-term challenges for [X] involved with a move, versus the long-term challenges for [X] if she doesn’t move. Is that a fair way to look at it? Yes.

And when you’re balancing those items, what I hear your evidence saying, this morning, is you don’t think it’s too late to give that transition a go. That’s a fair comment – summary? Yes.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.40 lines 13–20)
In relation to time between the child and the mother in the event the child was placed in the father’s care, the single expert was asked whether the mother could practically support the orders moving forward, to which she answered:
I don’t – I don’t think it is the practical. I think it is the – the messages that she is sending to [X] which are the issue, and they are not necessarily verbal messages, but you know, whether it is overt or covert, the message is that her father is a dangerous person, and, “You need to be cautious”, and those messages I think what are important for the mother, and I don’t know whether she can do that. I don’t know if she can do that.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.41 lines 25–30)
The single expert said there needed to be about a three-month period of no time with the mother to enable a reset if the child was to be placed in the father’s care and that time thereafter with the mother would need to occur initially on a supervised basis and that only time would tell if unsupervised time could be possible. In relation to the child’s engagement with various therapists, the single expert opined that whilst the child had been given the opportunity to engage with multiple therapists, the child has not been given the opportunity to have the independence to be able to do so because if the therapists narrative does not accord with the mother’s view, then the mother ends therapy. The single expert was asked questions about the supervision report arising from a visit in August 2025 which recorded as follows:
I asked why they continued attending if they did not enjoy them. And [X] explained to them there are ongoing legal proceedings that their mother is involved in, and she is preparing a lot of documentation. So evidence – so attending these visits ensures there is sufficient evidence.
Do you recall reading that?---Yes, I do.

And to your mind, is that not a blatant example of [X] understanding her own position as to be someone on the mother’s team who is collecting evidence? ---It is a blatant example of intentionally involving the child for the purpose of gaining leverage.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.50 lines 1–12)
She was also asked some questions about another supervision report of 17 November 2025 and said:
Well, the timing of that is interesting in that, you know, does she feel that she doesn’t need to attend any more because the evidence is already presented to the court. Therefore, we have completed the task. That would be a concern given that the litigation is still ongoing and that has already commenced that disengagement from the court orders.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.62 lines 34–38)
In relation to whether it was too late, the expert opined as follows:
And it’s not a case where the court can just say, this is all too hard, is it?---No, it’s not.

Because it’s not too late for [X], is it? ---It’s not too late. Her concerns for her mental health and wellbeing in the future, I think, are very important and it’s not too late for that to change.

And to your mind, the way – I beg your pardon – to your mind the balance of risk for [X] lies in favour of changing her residence, even though that might create a short term problems for her? ---We do need to balance those risks, don’t we, that – and I have noted that in my report that the risks are in the short term, she will experience distress in her father’s care. But then she may then settled. However, if she remains in her mother’s care, the status quo will remain. And then where does that leave her as a functional adolescent and adult moving forward with peers, with relationships, is my concern.

(Transcript 24 November 2025, p.63 lines 5–19)

DISCUSSION

Each of the parties and the ICL filed an Outline of Case document identifying the affidavits on which they intended to rely. Each of the mother, father, and the father’s partner were cross-examined as was the single expert. The father was granted leave to rely on the affidavit of Ms R, the court-ordered therapist, and its annexures.
I have read all the material relied upon including the exhibits. Whilst the evidence raised a series of issues, I am not required to address every fact or submission made (Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48; [2003] 200 ALR 447).
Each of the parties sought various factual findings.
In the case of the mother, she sought findings set out in Exhibit 112 which records as follows:
By engaging in “silent treatment” in his relationship with the Mother, as accepted by the Father in cross-examination, the Father engaged in psychological abuse of the Mother.
By engaging in psychological abuse of the Mother, the Father perpetrated family violence on the Mother.
The Father’s conduct and correspondence with the Mother following the making of the 01 03 2018 Orders was aggressive and hostile.
Such aggressive and hostile communication and conduct caused or contributed to a continuation of the conflict between the parties after the making of the 0l 03 2018 Orders.
The matters stated at 1 - 4 above caused or contributed to a reluctance of the part of the Mother to communicate with the Father about parenting issues for [X].
As admitted by the Father in cross-examination, the Mother’s actions in pausing the commencement of overnight time for [X] upon the Mother learning of the existence of the Father’s flatmate were reasonable and protective of [X], not part of a campaign intended at alienating the Father from [X’s] life.
Despite any finding as to the outcome of such actions, in seeking and arranging therapeutic support for [X], the Mother was motivated by concerns for [X’s] mental health and wellbeing, not part of a campaign intended at alienating the Father from [X’s] life.
The Father’s conduct in withdrawing his consent to [X] attending upon the school counsellor in December 2024 and in obstructing the arranging of mental health support for [X] following the making of the 06 12 2024 Orders was not child-focused and not in [X’s] best interests.
Despite any finding as to the cause or causes of such symptoms, [X] has engaged in skin-picking and other acts of self-harm and has expressed suicidal ideation when contemplating the possibility of the Court making the orders sought by the Father, for [X] to live with the Father.
In the event that the Court makes the orders sought by the Father, there is a risk of significant deterioration in [X’s] mental health.
In light of the admissions by each of the Father and [Ms ZZ] in cross-examination that [X] does not discuss “big emotions” with either of them, there is a significant risk that [X] will not make the Father or [Ms ZZ] aware of any deterioration in their mental health should the Court make the orders sought by the Father.
The Father has no realistic plan to manage the transition of [X] into living in his fulltime care.
In making the orders sought by the Father, there is a risk that [X] will endeavour to self-place back with the Mother and/ or otherwise run away, such that conflict and litigation between the parties is highly likely to recur.
In light of 9 - 13 above, there is an unacceptable risk to [X’s] mental and/ or physical health in making the orders sought by the Father.
...
[X’s] relationship with the Father and knowledge of the paternal family can be maintained through the orders the Mother seeks for the Father to have identity contact in the form of unsupervised time on or around special occasions, and letters, card and gifts.
The parents have no demonstrated capacity to hold joint major long-term decisionmaking responsibility, such that an order ought to be made for the parent with whom [X] is to primarily live to hold sole major long-term decision-making responsibility, save that the parents ought to hold joint decision-making responsibility in relation to [X’s] name.
The father sought factual findings set out in Exhibit 113, which has been consequentially renumbered but the text of which records as follows:

At the time of trial, [X] presents as a highly anxious and traumatised pre-adolescent
[X] is aligned and enmeshed with the mother
[X] is not capable of comprehending the long term consequences of a loss of relationship with the father
[X]’ current expressed wishes not to spend time with her father should be given little weight
The father does not pose an unacceptable risk to [X]

The father and [X] have previously enjoyed a healthy and attached relationship post separation
The father has capacity to support [X] living with him, including the capacity to support [X’s] psychological needs
10. The father has the capacity to support [X’s] relationship with the mother
