The factual findings sought by each party can be broadly summarised as giving rise to the following issues for consideration which will be addressed in these reasons:
(1) Whether the father perpetrated family violence upon the mother; whether the father post-separation engaged in conduct that was aggressive and hostile; whether such aggressive and hostile conduct caused or contributed to the conflict post the 2018 orders; whether the family violence and the aggressive and hostile conduct caused or contributed to the mother’s reluctance to communicate with the father about parenting issues (mother’s Factual Findings 1–5; father’s Factual Finding 7)

(2) The mother’s parenting capacity (mother’s Factual Finding 6–7; father’s Factual Findings 1–3 and 11–16)

(3) The father’s parenting capacity (mother’s Factual Finding 8; father’s Factual Finding 8–10)

(4) Issues of unacceptable risk (mother’s Factual Finding 9–14; father’s Factual Finding 6, 8 and 17)

(5) The child’s views (father’s Factual Finding 4 and 5)

(6) What orders are in the child’s best interests (mother’s Factual Finding 16 and 17)

A substantial part of the mother’s affidavit (which ran to 1,122 paragraphs) was her assertion that the father was a perpetrator of family violence both during the relationship and after separation. Examples in her affidavit included the following:
Over time, I came to realise that such actions were not casual or incidental. They were part of what I experienced to be as a pattern of emotional abuse and coercive control, which undermined my self-worth, created in me a sense of insecurity and inadequacy, reinforced my dependence on [Mr Kirill’s] approval, and kept me competing for his attention. I experienced [Mr Kirill’s] actions as a calculated power play, which destabilised me emotionally, eroded my confidence, and kept me feeling disempowered and isolated within the relationship.
...
I came to experience my relationship with [Mr Kirill] as one of domestic violence and coercive control, which I say has persisted since our separation.

...
Throughout our relationship, [Mr Kirill] consistently demonstrated a volatile and unpredictable temperament. At any given moment, a harmless comment or benign interaction could escalate into disproportionate anger. Once triggered, there was no effective way for me to de-escalate the situation; neither calm reasoning nor emotional appeals had any impact. This created an ongoing sense of instability and fear for me living in the household. I felt like 1 was constantly “walking on eggshells”, not knowing what might set [Mr Kirill] off or when.
These episodes were followed by prolonged periods of silence, during which [Mr Kirill] refused to speak to me for weeks, not even saying hello or goodbye, and blocking all communication by turning off his phone. He would either lock himself in a spare bedroom or avoid coming home.
The intensity of [Mr Kirill’s] anger was so overwhelming that it permeated the entire environment. His silence was accompanied by contemptuous and disdainful looks, leaving me feeling emotionally isolated and psychologically punished. I found such behaviour to be profoundly destabilising,
Although [Mr Kirill] did not strike me directly, he used intimidation and psychological manipulation and later threats to keep me in fear. At times, I found myself wishing that he would express his anger in another form such as physical aggression because the emotional toll of the silent treatment for weeks on end was so intense, it felt more painful and dehumanising than a swift act of violence.
I lived in a constant state of hypervigilance, never knowing when the next outburst and withdrawal would occur, or how long it would last. The persistent unpredictability and emotional withholding significantly eroded my sense of wellbeing and security. When [Mr Kirill] eventually resumed communication, I felt a combination of relief at the temporary return to normalcy, deep caution, and emotional exhaustion from enduring the cycle of abuse.
These patterns were not isolated incidents, but part of a broader dynamic of coercive control that left me feeling emotionally unsafe in my own home, where my psychological safety was dependent on [Mr Kirill’s] mood, which created a profound power imbalance in our relationship.
Under a heading, “Gaslighting and emotional manipulation” she recorded the following:
The last statement - “I’m not hurting you. I’m not even laying a finger on you” - was said with what I observed to be a smugness in [Mr Kirill’s] tone of voice and facial expression, which conveyed to me [Mr Kirill’s] belief in his cleverness in being able to inflict harm on me without physical violence. To me, that encapsulated [Mr Kirill’s] pattern of psychological and emotional abuse, where harm was inflicted through control, manipulation and emotional cruelty.
Under a heading, “Verbal abuse and taunting” she stated:
[Mr Kirill] also subjected me to a persistent pattern of daily denigration, repeated derogatory taunts and belittling, often disguised as humour, living with him felt akin to living with a schoolyard bully, chipping away at my self-esteem. I received a constant daily stream of mockery, criticism or put-downs over my physical appearance (even the shape of my nose), my abilities and life choices. When I showed signs of hurt or discomfort, [Mr Kirill] then ridiculed me and gaslit me for being “too sensitive”.
...
The daily stream of mocking and put-downs eroded my self-esteem. As I lost my sense of self [Mr Kirill] grew more powerful in our relationship dynamic.
Other headings with various asserted examples included but were not limited to “financial abuse”, “[Mr Kirill’s] control” and “[Mr Kirill’s] abuse of me during our attempts to conceive a child”.
The single expert recorded the mother reporting amongst other matters that the father during the relationship isolated her from others, made derogatory comments about her to her friends and others, criticised her career choices, flirted with other women, was controlling, was volatile and rageful, that it was not safe to say things to him, that he engaged in gaslighting her and projected these behaviours onto the child, that he emotionally manipulated the child and was financially abusive to the mother.
In her cross-examination, the mother agreed she had reported to various health professionals that she had been told the father had a gun, had threatened to kill her, and threatened to abduct the child. She reported to the police that she had been told that the father would seek to “frame” her boyfriend for sexual abuse of the child and she agreed in cross-examination she believed the child had been “a victim of child sexual abuse at the hands of the father” (Transcript 18 November 2025, p.16 line 13).
The mother also asserted the child had been exposed to family violence. The single expert recorded the mother reporting the following:
When asked what her thoughts were on what was causing [X’s] suicidal ideation and self-harming behaviour, [Ms Tevye] reported that [X’s] exposure to family violence by her father, his coercive controlling behaviour, being given the silent treatment, body shaming, being put down, and being with a parent who has emotion dysregulation, have all contributed. [Ms Tevye’s] recollection is that [X] started to pick her skin in October 2023 when she experienced a worry feeling when present with her father, and suicidal thoughts such as “If I wasn’t born...” or “what if I fell off [the balcony], all this bad stuff would not be happening”. [Ms Tevye] said that there were two incidences where [Mr Kirill] allegedly hit and threatened [X] in response to her skin picking, specifically he hit her leg and said “stop” in a loud voice, and the second occasion he grabbed her hand and said, “pick, pick, pick” and used her hand to hit her face and said, “hit, hit, hit”. He also said to her that if her skin picking continued “I’m going to hit you”. [Ms Tevye] recalled that the self-harming behaviour settled at the end of July 2024...
The child reported, to their treaters and to the single expert, behaviour by the father that included the father hitting or pushing their hand away when picking their skin on one occasion, had used their hand to hit their face, had yelled at them, had scared them, that he practised ‘silent treatment’ on them and restricted their access to food. Ms WW reported that the child was terrified of the father, that they felt isolated, ignored and denigrated in the care of the father.
The Mother’s Outline of Case document recorded:
The Mother’s evidence is that she and [X] experienced pervasive psychological abuse and coercive & controlling behaviour by the Father, which has continued post-separation – directed towards both the Mother, in the context of the parties’ attempts to co-parent, and towards [X] during their time and communication with the Father.
Further, [X] reported to both the Mother and the single expert to have been physically abused by the Father.
The mother’s counsel submitted as follows:
MS RUSITI: Yes. I mean, I assert that the father’s engaging in silent treatment of the mother during the relationship was a form of psychological abuse, and was coercive and controlling. And was, therefore, family violence. I accept that the evidence does not go – in terms of the timeframe, does not go beyond that timeframe.

(Transcript 25 November 2025, p.36 lines 34–48)
The mother’s counsel conceded that the evidence did not support a finding of coercive or controlling conduct once the relationship ended. In that respect, the parties separated in May 2015.
Why the mother’s affidavit addressed the above matters and others at such extraordinary length and in such extraordinary detail was unexplained particularly where, with the exception of some questions as to the father’s engagement with a psychologist in the context of “silent treatment” and some questions directed to correspondence, none of the matters were addressed in cross-examination by the mother’s counsel nor was there a testing of his denials.
Remarkably and despite the content of the Outline of Case document, her affidavit, or what she reported to the single expert, the police or the numerous health professionals the mother attended upon, no factual finding was sought nor was a submission made that the father had exposed the child to silent treatment or that the child was at risk of physical or sexual harm or the victim of family or domestic violence. Nor did the allegations of family violence form any part of the submissions as to unacceptable risk. One might speculate that such a submission was not advanced as it was irreconcilable with the unchallenged expert evidence.
The father’s affidavit addressed the mother’s assertions that he engaged in silent treatment as a form of psychological abuse as follows:
The Applicant has previously asserted that I would regularly give her the “silent” treatment and that this was a form of abuse towards her or that I was prone to fits of anger. I deny these assertions. I am an introverted person and do not like confrontation. I do not recall occasions where I shouted during our relationship. I experienced anxiety and panic attacks, especially during stressful life events such as moving countries and changes of work, but with the help of ongoing therapy, I have developed strategies to assist me during these moments.
I deny that I subjected the Applicant to “silent treatment.”
...
The Applicant continues to assert that she is fearful of me. At no time has there been any ADVO issued against me. I have never engaged in any form of intimidation, harassment, threats, nor ever assaulted the Applicant.
The father in his affidavit denied being angry with the child and denied giving them the silent treatment. The father during cross-examination denied he had used the child’s hand to hit their face or had pushed or hit their hand away when they were picking their skin saying he “moved her hand away” (transcript 21 November 2025, p.43 line 42).
In the context of coercive or controlling conduct, Aldridge and Carew JJ observed in Pickford & Pickford [2024] FedCFamC1A 249; (2024) FLC 94-230 (“Pickford & Pickford”) as follows:
When determining an allegation that a person has engaged in behaviour that coerces or controls a family member, a trial judge will undertake a forensic examination of all relevant evidence to:
(a) identify the behaviour about which complaint is made;

(b) identify the full context of the behaviour including any explanation that may be given by the alleged perpetrator;

(c) identify the impact of the behaviour on the alleged victim (mere assertion by the alleged victim that they feel coerced or controlled is insufficient);

(d) make all relevant factual findings; and

(e) explain why the behaviour in question is or is not family violence that coerces or controls the family member and if the alleged behaviour does not entail a course or pattern of conduct, explain how the behaviour can nevertheless be characterised as behaviour that coerces or controls, if so found.

No part of the submissions advanced on behalf of the mother chose to address any aspect of the task to be undertaken in accordance with the above requirements. The Court was simply asked to conclude that it was so because the mother said so and her counsel so submitted. Their Honour’s paragraph cannot be read as requiring a court to embark on such a journey unaided by submission as to the relevant conduct, its impact and why it constitutes family violence where the party urging such a finding fails to do so. It might glibly be observed that where the party does not do that which is required of them, they cannot be later heard to complain if the Court adopts the same approach.
An allegation of family violence is just like any other allegation of fact. The person asserting, in this case the mother, bears the onus of proof (Pickford & Pickford at [79]). Section 140(1) of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) requires the Court to find the case of a party proved if it is satisfied that the case has been proved on the balance of probabilities. This can be informed by ‘an actual persuasion of the fact’; inconsistencies may be resolved by evidence that is more probable than not or evidence that is consistent with incontrovertible facts or compelling inferences; by the adducing of compelling evidence casting doubt on the denial, by adducing evidence of an admission, by the tender of documents casting doubt upon or inconsistent with the denial, through cross-examination or by an acceptance of one parties’ evidence in preference to that of another based on some or all of the above in weighting the whole of the evidence.
The sole area of cross-examination on this issue was some questions about the father’s engagement with a psychologist in 2012 and the tender of the clinical notes of the psychologist (Exhibit 95). I am not persuaded that the clinical notes demonstrate the falsity or implausibility of the father’s case, give rise to an inconsistency or incontrovertible fact, nor the drawing of a compelling inference. If it were, there should have been a submission demonstrative of the assertion.
In the clinical notes is an email from the mother to the psychologist dated 28 March 2012. It records in part the following:
My husband reacts to issues in his life by shutting down, not talking & isolating. This can go on for weeks at a time. Not only does this not allow hm to work through an issue effectively but to be honest it is very toxic to our marriage. It is not only the grief that comes from infertility that causes him to react this way, any issues we have between us. It has been a behaviour he has used since his childhood so unfortunately is pretty entrenched.

(Exhibit 95)
Behaviour that might be toxic to a marriage does not establish that it meets the definition of family violence.
Exhibit 95 refers to a session on 25 May 2012 which records the father reporting, “long period of silences when feeling misunderstood”. He admitted saying it to the psychologist, but he was not asked what the entry might mean in the context of the mother. It was put to him that a reference to “silent mode” was what the mother described, and he said not necessarily so. He was asked whether one of the things he discussed with the psychologist was “shutting down and not communicating in work and personal relationships” and he responded that it was part of the treatment. There was no cross-examination of when this occurred or that it was done to the mother to warrant some admission consistent with the mother’s case.
The notes of the same session record under the heading, “Goals/Preferred Outcome” the following:
Work on personal issues, take responsibility myself, look at avoidance strategies (addictions, infidelity, work, moving, constant change) particularly in regard[s] to intimate relationships, afraid of becoming like his dad).

(Exhibit 95)
The next in time note addressing their relationship is one dated 13 September 2012 which records, “[Mr Kirill] Recognises he’s very reactive in relationships, gets in the way, as he doesn’t talk about it, trying to change that pattern discuss things as they happen eg. Of doing this with [Ms Tevye] in week-end”, while the last session dated 20 September 2012 records “Relationship is better with [Ms Tevye] – common goal, she’s more accepting of their situation now, less arguing – [Mr Kirill] discussing issues before they escalate, listening non-judgmentally to each other”.
At its highest, the notes and the mother’s email refer to a problem which the mother described as toxic to their relationship. As much may be true. But not all behaviours or conduct, even ones that may be described as unpleasant, boorish or insensitive can be pathologized as family violence let alone constitute conduct that had the consequence of coercing or controlling one’s partner. I do not accept it has been established that the “silent treatment” was perpetrated in a persistent manner or used as a form of punishment.
As the Full Court observed in Stokey & Dye (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1A 239:
...No court is compelled to accept as correct the unchallenged or uncontradicted evidence of a witness (GLJ v Trustees of the Roman Catholic Church for the Diocese of Lismore [2023] HCA 32; (2023) 280 CLR 442 at [60] and [71]; HML v The Queen [2008] HCA 16; (2008) 235 CLR 334 at [130]; Louth v Diprose [1992] HCA 61; (1992) 175 CLR 621 at 624–625 and 635–636; Ellis v Wallsend District Hospital (1989) 17 NSWLR 553 at 587–588).
To accept the mother’s case in the face of the denial requires an acceptance of her evidence. I found the mother to be an unimpressive and unreliable witness for the following reasons:
(a) She agreed in cross-examination that she has mislead health professionals and the police by representing that the father had a mental illness when she knew that Dr UU had made no such diagnosis. She agreed she had misrepresented to the child’s school the Court orders made in 2018 by suggesting that the fathers time was supervised when it was not and where the requirement for someone to be initially present was to protect the child and the father from allegations made by the mother.

(b) Her evidence as to her belief as to whether the child had been sexually abused by the father changed over the course of her oral evidence such that I accept the father’s submission that her evidence on that issue was tainted by untruths. In that respect on the first day of her cross-examination she said she had never held the view that the child was at risk of sexual abuse in the father’s care. This was inconsistent with police records which recorded that in January 2016 the mother took the child to RR Hospital as she was concerned that the child had been sexually assaulted by the father (Exhibit 5, page 75). This was also inconsistent with representations she made to BA Mental Health Services where the mother reported to the centre that one of her concerns was sexual abuse of the child by the father and is irreconcilable with the counselling notes with Ms AS which record the mother not understanding how FACS/JIRT could have made a decision that the sexual assault allegations were inconclusive (Exhibit 16).

(c) Despite saying on the first day of cross-examination that she had never held the view that the child was at risk of sexual abuse in the care of the father, on the second day of her cross-examination she said that she genuinely thinks the child is a victim of sexual abuse at the hands of the father.

(d) The mother’s statement in cross examination and actions in taking the child to the hospital in January 2016 and informing the police that she did that because she was concerned the child had been sexually assaulted by the father is contrary to her solicitors letter a month later which records “our client wishes make it clear she did not allege that your client had in fact done anything...” (Exhibit 8).

(e) The mother was asked about a report to the police in June 2018 by her and the father’s former partner, Ms AU. The mother initially said she dropped Ms AU at the police station, but that Ms AU went in by herself. The mother later admitted that she had not just dropped Ms AU of but had also walked into the police station. The story then changed to her not driving her there but merely meeting her there but later agreed she had dropped her at the police station. I found the mother’s responses in relation to the visits to the police by her and Ms AU as unconvincing and dissembling.

(f) The mother was cross-examined about a cruise the father took with the child in January 2019. The mother agreed that she had told her counsellor after the child returned from a cruise that she had concerns that the child slept in the same bed as the father on the cruise despite the child telling her there was a bunk bed. I am satisfied that the mother reported this to her counsellor for the purposes of generating a mandatory disclosure notwithstanding having been told that it had not happened.

(g) She was also asked questions about an allegation that a friend of the father’s had told her that the father had been talking about killing the mother and abducting the child. She reported this to her counsellor. The mother denied that she made this report to her counsellor to generate a child protection report. I do not accept the mother’s denial. The mother initially said that she genuinely thought that the father had made a threat to take her life. She was then asked the following question:

I am going to suggest, [Ms Tevye], that if you had any genuine concern that the father was going to abduct [X] or kill you, you would have expressed that concern to the police at the time. Do you agree or disagree with that? ---Yes, I agree.

(Transcript 18 November 2025, p.32 lines 32 – 34)

I am satisfied that the mother never genuinely believed the allegation the father was going to kill her and/or abduct the child and that it was reported, despite her denial, for the sole purpose of generating a child protection report.
(h) It was put to the mother several times that her reasons for not returning to see Ms M was because she had a problem with Ms M. The mother denied the proposition and said it was the child who had the problem with Ms M. She was then shown an entry in the notes of Ms WW recording the mother’s complaints about Ms M and only after further questioning admitting that it was because she had a problem with Ms M’s ethics as well as the child’s views of Ms M.
A witness gives an oath or affirmation to tell the whole truth, not part of the truth. A witness who tells part of the truth is as untruthful as one who tells a complete untruth. Taking all the evidence into account including the absence of any probative cross-examination, the father’s denials, my findings that the mother was an unconvincing and unreliable witness and whose evidence I find was in parts untruthful, means I am not persuaded that the mother has discharged the burden of proof. It was the mother’s case to prove, and I am not persuaded she has done so to the requisite standard. I am not satisfied that the evidence establishes the father engaged in psychological abuse of the mother or that he perpetrated family violence on the mother.
Nor do I accept the submission that post-separation the father engaged in a pattern of aggressive, hostile and threatening behaviour. In submissions, the mother’s counsel was asked to identify the evidence to underpin the submission. The evidence was said to be found in Exhibits 89–92. Exhibits 89 and 90 are emails sent on 6 March 2018 while Exhibit 91 and 92 are emails sent by the father on 1 April 2020. I do not accept that several emails on one day in 2018 and other emails two years later constitutes a pattern of aggressive, hostile or threatening behaviour. Nor do I accept that other correspondence not referred to in submissions establishes that he engaged in a pattern of aggressive, hostile and threatening behaviour.
The father agreed in hindsight there were things he said that should not have been said. On at least two occasions he conceded that an assertion of contravention had no basis. It was not however established that at the time of making the assertion he knew it had no foundation. The bringing of an application for contravention or even the foreshadowing of it is neither improper or contrary to the Act or Rules of Court. It becomes improper when it is done for an improper purpose. I am not persuaded that the mother has established that it was done for an improper purpose.
In the context of the child’s asserted fears and the mother’s repeated assertion she has been subjected to family violence there is a disconnect between the child’s relationship with the father as reported by the father and his partner up until about mid-2024 and that reported by the child to the single expert, the mother, and the child’s treaters.
The father gave detailed evidence in his affidavit at paragraphs 38, 39, 178–187, 189–210 of the nature of his relationship with the child and his experience of them in his home. He reports that until July 2024 the child was:
...a gregarious, happy, bright, fun-loving and chatty little girl. She was intelligent, curious about the world, loved animals, and enjoyed imaginative play and stories. She had a creative flair, enjoyed playing outside, and was naturally social and outgoing with friends.
He did not observe them locking themself in their room. He says that he and his partner in 2024 started looking for a home to live in and that the child was excited about it, “especially those with interesting rooms for her, big back yards to play in, and if they had a pool” (affidavit, paragraph 83). He says of the child:
During this time, I considered [X] to be a well-adjusted child who was thriving socially and academically, despite the ongoing conflict between her parents. I was not aware of the extent to which the Applicant had begun what I now understand to be “expert shopping” with [X], raising new allegations and involving her with a range of practitioners. It was during this period, in late 2023, that I first noticed [X] beginning to engage in skin-picking behaviour.
In cross-examination by the ICL, he described the child between 2018 and 2024 in the following terms:
In the period between 2018 and 2024, how would you describe [X] when she was with you? ---Very happy, content, precocious, fun-loving, into pranks, big humour, love to watch movies, that kind of child. Who’s also very good academically in school, wants to do public speaking, be in drama. That’s how she was.

(Transcript 21 November 2025, p.72 lines 1–5)
His partner was asked questions about her observation of the father’s relationship with the child. She said she had never seen him yell at them nor had she seen the child afraid of him. She described a relationship where the child would come into their bedroom in the morning and “snuggle with her dad on her dad’s side of the bed” and she said she saw the child reach “for her dad’s hand and hold onto it” when they went on walks.
The father and his partner were not challenged on their evidence about the nature of his relationship with the child up to that point in time. I accept the father and his partner’s account of his relationship with the child. I accept the observations of the single expert that the child’s reactions to the father are disproportionate to the reported events (Report, paragraph 159). I am satisfied that as reported by health professional who took a more objective view such as Ms M and Ms U that the child’s themes were like the mothers in detail and vocabulary.
I do not accept the mother’s repeated assertions to numerous health professionals and others including the police that the child has been the victim of family violence perpetrated by the father. In that respect, I note Ms M reporting to the single expert the mother’s view that the child was being traumatised while Ms M and Ms U did not perceive there were any actual risk of harm issues in the father’s care.
I am not persuaded that the father engaged in coercive or controlling behaviour towards the child, or that he sexually or physically assaulted the child, or that the child has been the victim of family and domestic violence perpetrated by the father. I accept the opinion of the single expert expressed in paragraph 160 of her report that the child’s rejection of the father is influenced by enmeshment with the mother, loyalty binds, and the father’s lack of attunement.
The parties reached an agreement in 2018 by way of final consent orders that followed the allegations of sexual assault in 2016, and the report prepared by Dr UU in 2017 (Exhibit 4).
The mother contends in her factual findings that the father’s method of communicating caused or contributed to interparty conflict post the making of the orders and caused a reluctance on the mother’s part to communicate with the father about parenting issues. I am not persuaded that the totality of that characterisation is borne out by the evidence. The interparty conflict was well established prior to the making of the 2018 orders. In that respect, the single expert reports and I accept the child has been exposed to conflict for almost their entire life.
Dr UU observed as follows:
There can be little doubt that [X] has been well aware of her parents’ ongoing discord and negative feelings for each other. As a toddler, her mother’s attitude to her father would have influence her reported “wariness of” him, while of course any negative personal experiences with him would have had a similar effect.

However, their marked difficulties continue post separation, which would have been distressing for her. Her increasingly agitated behaviours at handovers in December 2015 could have arisen from the parental hostility to each other at these times, as well as from her very limited time with her father and the need to separate from him.

The couple relationship clearly became noxious to both of [X’s] parents, with each blaming the other for the lack of support, kindness and understanding they experienced during their marriage. They have alleged the other has lied and perpetrated abusive behaviours and they remain severely fearful and/or intimidated by the other.

(Exhibit 4)
Dr UU under the heading, “Living Arrangements” stated:
In my opinion, the advantages to [X] of establishing a secure relationship with her father through regular, safe, nurturing time with him outweigh the significant disadvantages of markedly limiting or ceasing their time together.

To protect both [X] and [Mr Kirill] from further allegations he is a risk of harm to his daughter, it would be a wise precaution by [Mr Kirill] to spend time with [X] while [Ms VV] is nearby (ie not formally supervising them) until [X] is around eight years old.
I find the mother sought to misrepresent that last paragraph to various health professionals and others presenting the father as the risk when it was clear that Dr UU was concerned that the risk lay with her. I am satisfied that both parties contributed to the conflict but in different ways. I do not accept that the mother was reluctant to raise issues with the father but rather was intent on controlling the narrative. In that respect, I accept the observations of Ms M who saw the parties from June 2018. The single expert recorded her views as follows:
...It was not until June 2018 that agreement was made between the parents for [Ms M] to see [X], whom she saw on an ad hoc basis. [Ms M] reported that there was significant parental conflict from the outset in which [X] was caught in the middle of the psychological aggression – with [Ms Tevye] having her own trauma that affected how she parented and [Mr Kirill] would refer to [Ms Tevye] as “the mother” and could not refer to [Ms Tevye] by name.
[Ms M] used a non-directive approach to therapy with [X] to give her space to talk about her feelings, however [Ms M] believed that [Ms Tevye] had given [X] an agenda and there was a constant need for [Ms Tevye] to direct the narrative of therapy. She recalled that “everything was an issue”, including who owned the school uniform, and so her work included working with the parents on how this impacted on [X]. At times [Ms M] saw [Mr Kirill] and addressed issues such as how to talk about [Ms Tevye], needing to say hello at changeover, and how that impacted [X].
The mother continued with such a narrative and controlling conduct thereafter.
The mother contends that the father’s proposals would expose the child to an unacceptable risk. As identified earlier, the mother does not submit that any aspect of family violence informs the submission of unacceptable risk. Until July 2024, the child was reporting to the mother and others suicidal ideation, was picking at their skin and harming themself. The single expert described the skin picking as a way by which the child managed their distress and was a coping mechanism. I accept that opinion. In relation to the suicidal ideation, the single expert opined that she did not believe the child ever had a plan or intent to carry out it but had some passive suicidal thoughts.
The evidence reveals that the child’s suicidal ideation, the skin picking and self-harming subsided following the suspension of time in July 2024, albeit the mother’s evidence is that there was a resurgence in November 2024, but it has not occurred in 2025 despite time resuming albeit in a very different configuration. There is clearly a risk that there may be a return to this behaviour if the child were placed in the father’s care. Likewise, the single expert opined that there was a risk of a deterioration in the child’s mental health by being placed in the father’s care in circumstances where the report from Ms M indicates that the child’s mental health is currently stable. Notwithstanding the alleged trauma that seems to be surrounding the child’s relationship with the father, the child is and always has done well at school. The reasons for the disconnect are not entirely or adequately explained.
The evidence reveals the child has not engaged with the father or Ms ZZ in discussing what is described by various health professionals as their “big emotions”. The expert agreed that there was a risk in the placement of the child in the father’s care that there may be no-one with whom they could discuss these “big emotions” particularly in circumstances where they did not seem to be engaging in any meaningful way in therapy with Ms M or would on the father’s case have a restricted relationship with the mother. A further risk is that if there is a deterioration in the child’s mental health and where they are not discussing their feelings or emotions with the father or his partner, there is a risk it goes unnoticed and untreated. That said, the child has in the past engaged with the school counsellor and it seems assumed by all parties there will be available at their chosen high school a school counsellor.
Another risk of placement in the father’s care identified by the mother’s counsel is the child self-places either by running away and returning to the mother or running away from the father to some other location. There is evidence this is something that the child has referred to in the past. The single expert agreed that the child would be very angry with the father and his partner and may remain angry and rejecting of them for a significant period.
I accept that the above raise a risk of harm based on the father’s proposals. It is impossible to quantify with any precision the likelihood of harm occurring but accept that it exists as a possibility. If any of the possibilities were to occur, then the magnitude of harm is high such that it would, in the absence of any ameliorating factors, be unacceptable.
I am however satisfied that there are ameliorating factors that move the risk from unacceptable to acceptable. In that respect, while the child is not engaging with Ms M, it is a regime that is in place and is supported by the father as opposed to how the mother responds to it. I accept the evidence of the father and his partner that there was until July 2024 a closeness in the relationship between the father and the child. Whilst the father has in the past erred in relation to his parenting responsibilities and acted on occasions inappropriately, on balance I am satisfied that the father does have the capacity to reflect and consider the interests of the child and is supportive of the child maintaining a relationship with the mother. I am satisfied that he and his partner are cognisant of the risks moving forward in relation to the proposals that they seek. I am satisfied that these ameliorating factors are such as to render the risk that does exist acceptable.
The father contends the child is at an unacceptable risk in the care of the mother. I am satisfied that the evidence establishes as identified by the single expert at paragraph 169 of her report that the mother does not support a meaningful relationship between the child and the father. I am satisfied that the mother has undermined any attempt at ongoing therapy as made clear in the evidence of Ms R. The report by Ms M to the single expert makes it clear that the mother brings the child to therapy through ‘gritted teeth’. I am satisfied from the supervision reports that the mother has engaged the child in the litigation process and has used the child for her own purposes in the litigation.
The single expert opines that the loss of a meaningful relationship with a biological parent can cause the child long-term distress, identity confusion and interpersonal difficulties. In the mother’s home, the child has an unbalanced view of the world which involves a complete rejection of the father. In that respect, I accept the evidence of the single expert that the rejection of the father reflects a coping mechanism rather than a response to a risk of harm.
In the primary care of the mother, the child is, as opined by the single expert, in an enmeshed environment that is psychologically harmful to her. The risks for the child moving forward are, as identified by the single expert, significant. They include reinforcing the child’s current distorted belief structure that the father poses a risk of harm to them and which may, as opined by the single expert at paragraph 168, cause long-term harm to their psychological well-being, their identity formation and their understanding of family relationships and risks permanently severing their relationship with the father and the extended paternal family. In the absence of a meaningful relationship, the expert opines that the child will grow up believing the father is abusive and harmful and without an opportunity to test or challenge the narrative would further entrench a distorted view of their family history.
I am satisfied that the above risks of harm are present and are continuing to occur and the magnitude of harm to the child is high. The mother presents no ameliorating circumstance that will reduce the risk of harm. Reducing the father’s time with the child is not an ameliorating circumstance but rather an exacerbating one. I am satisfied that leaving this child in the care of the mother is to expose the child to a continuing risk of harm which constitutes an unacceptable risk.
I address below such of the factors pursuant to s 60CC of the Act as are relevant to my determination and which have not otherwise been considered.

s 60CC(2)(a) – what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:

(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child)

This has been addressed extensively above. I am satisfied that on balance the arrangement that promotes the safety of the child is one where they are in the father’s primary care.

s 60CC(2)(b) – any views expressed by the child

The ICL submitted the Court should give significant weight to the child’s views. I find that submission difficult to comprehend in circumstances where the child says they are afraid of the father and does not want to spend time with him but the ICL invites in submissions to conclude that there are no safety issues in relation to the child spending unsupervised time with the father and proposes orders contrary to the child’s views for time on the first Sunday of each month unsupervised between 9.00 am and 5.00 pm.
In relation to the question of the child’s views, the single expert reported as follows:
The parental relationship has been marked by entrenched hostility, to which [X] has been repeatedly exposed. Both parents describe highly distressing handover experiences — [Ms Tevye] reporting [X] clinging to her, and [Mr Kirill] likening the changeovers to “prisoner exchanges.” These accounts suggest an environment of emotional turmoil that has had a substantial impact on [X’s] psychological development. [X’s] presentation appears indicative of a history of maladaptive coping strategies to deal with the significant generalised anxiety and distress she is feeling in the context of the parental conflict, which she is acutely aware of, and has ultimately affected her relationships and views about her parents. There is no evidence to suggest a psychotic disorder or an eating disorder; and a diagnosis such as Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) may be considered if her mental health and environmental factors settle, and only then to be considered if broader patterns across settings are evident, as it would currently not be an accurate assessment given the history of trauma that [X] has experienced.
[X] has stated that she no longer wants to have a relationship or contact with her father. She cites feeling fearful of her father in light of her experience of him being cruel and misattuned of her needs. [Mr Kirill] denies the allegations that he gave [X] the “silent treatment,” restricted her diet, or physically assaulted her. Health professionals who have approached the case from a more systemic perspective have noted that [X’s] emotional responses to her father appear disproportionate to the events described and they have not identified risk of harm from the father. [X] was also interviewed by DCJ and it appears that no substantiation of harm has been noted.
I have spoken with [X’s] school and several health professionals who have been involved with the family since the consent orders were made in 2018. Each therapist’s brief has been different, all have raised concerns regarding [X’s] emotional wellbeing, however their opinions differ as to the underlying factors contributing to her presentation. The nature of [X’s] relationship with her mother and whether [X] has the capacity to independently engage in therapy in the manner that she has with therapists has been questioned. There are concerns noted by [Ms M], who saw the whole family, about an enmeshed relationship between [X] and her mother, as well as concerns raised by [Ms PP] and [Ms U] about [X] finding it difficult to form independent thoughts or express feelings that may differ from her mother, misinterpreting her father’s behaviour, or disproportionately alarmed to minor parenting issues. Another area of concern raised has been [X’s] use of language and whether certain expressions are developmentally appropriate or not. In my view, gifted children or children without siblings can exhibit mature language use, however, the use of terms such as “guilt tripping” at the age of seven is not typical for a child of that age and suggests external influence through conversations beyond her developmental level. Given the above, it is difficult to place significant weight on the views of those practitioners who have not considered the systemic and complex dynamics of this matter.
...
At 11 years of age, [X’s] cognitive development is still characterised by concrete thinking. In the context of parental conflict, this can lead to oversimplified judgments such as one parent can be “all bad” and the other can be “all good” and she does not have a nuanced understanding of her family dynamics. Understandably, [X] is a young child whose distress is significant and she does not have the capacity to manage such turmoil – and she should not have to. [X] does not yet possess the maturity to comprehend the long-term implications of her expressed views as she relies on trusted adults (such as parents, teachers, and therapists) to help her challenge and consider alternative perspectives.

These above mentioned concerns raise the need for caution before attributing significant weight to her expressed wishes, particularly if there is no substantiated risk of harm in a parent’s care.
I accept the single expert’s opinion as to the weight to be given to the child’s expressed views. As referred to earlier, I am satisfied that there is not a risk of harm to the child arising from the father’s conduct and/or behaviour towards the child. I find that the child’s stage of development “is still characterised by concrete thinking” and that the child does “not have a nuanced understanding of her family dynamics” nor does the child have “the maturity to comprehend the long-term implications of her expressed views” (Report, paragraph 157).
Ms M told the single expert that the mother has given the child a narrative and there was a need for the mother to direct the therapy. I accept that evidence. I also find that there is a strong enmeshment and the mother’s views and feelings influence and impact negatively on the child and impede their ability to have an independent view of the father and that it would be difficult for the child to express a view or consider an alternative view of the father different to that held by the mother. For these reasons, while I recognise the child holds these views, I do not attribute significant weight to them.

s 60CC(2)(c) – the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child

The single expert describes the child as anxious, confused and traumatised. I accept that evidence. The single expert holds significant concerns for the child’s mental health and the prognosis for their future wellbeing is poor if the current circumstances continue (Report, paragraph 152). The single expert describes the conflict the child has been exposed to, both before and after separation, as having had a “clear and cumulative impact on her psychological wellbeing” (Report, paragraph 152).
It is apparent from the evidence that a continuation of the current regime of time is inconsistent with the child’s best interests. Consequently, I am satisfied that the child’s psychological and emotional needs require a change in the way they have been parented to date. The child’s developmental, psychological and emotional needs would ideally be met by having a meaningful relationship with both of their parents actively engaged in their life. The sad melancholic fact is the parents are incapable of co-parenting and consequently the outcome for this child is that they will have a relationship with one parent and a significantly lesser relationship with the other parent.

s 60CC(2)(d) – the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

Each of the parents seek an order for sole decision-making responsibility. Neither parent nor the ICL proposes that there would be a continuation of shared decision-making responsibility.
The parents have no ability to co-parent. Thus, it inevitably follows that the parent who has primary care of the child will have sole decision-making responsibility.
Each of the parents are compromised in their capacity to provide for the child’s needs. Each have contributed to the conflict that has led to them having a child who is traumatised.
I find the mother sees no value in the child having a relationship with the father and has actively undermined the child/father relationship. I do not accept the submission that the father’s pattern of interacting with the mother caused or created in the mother a reluctance to communicate with the father about parenting issues as addressed earlier.
I have no doubt that the mother deeply loves her child. The single expert described her as a caring, attentive, and fiercely protective parent. I have no doubt that the mother provides for the child’s practical needs. However, she is completely blinded when it comes to considering any benefit to the child in having a relationship with the father or any need on her part to encourage it.
The expert describes Ms M reporting the mother, whilst compliant with attending therapy sessions, brought the child through ‘gritted teeth’. The single expert expressed that as an unsurprising conclusion. The evidence of Ms R, the court-ordered therapist, was that the mother, “is not interested or motivated to attend family therapy sessions nor to encourage the relationship between the daughter X and her father” (annexure to Ms R’s affidavit filed 28 October 2025, p.7). I accept that evidence. To that end, the mother has failed to engage in the therapy the court ordered.
I accept the opinion of the single expert where she observed the mother does not support a meaningful relationship between the child and the father and could not outline any significant role that the father could or should have in the future.
I am satisfied that at least from the end of 2023 if not earlier the mother was focussed on gathering evidence for the purposes of suspending time between the child and the father which culminated in her letter to the father dated 15 July 2024 (Exhibit 59). To that end, the mother arranged for the child to see therapists without the father’s knowledge and in breach of her parental responsibility obligations. The numerous attendances upon therapists and the encouraging of mandatory reports were done with the aim of ending the relationship. With the knowledge of what the child was reporting to her and the child’s declining mental health, including reports of alleged suicidal ideation, the mother failed to contact the father. I reject entirely any notion that her failure to do so was protective or because of a fear of his reaction. I find that to be an artifice confected to justify her behaviour. Her conduct in not informing the father exposed the child to a continuing risk of harm of which he was entirely unaware and done entirely to suit her own ends.
The continuing narrative the child is exposed to in the mother’s household is that the father is abusive and a risk of harm is destructive of and incompatible with the child’s psychological and emotional needs. The risk of harm is not borne out by the evidence. I accept the single expert’s evidence that the mother has imposed on the child a view that they are in danger which could have the effect of creating for the child a view that the world is a scary place and men are scary. I accept her opinion that this is detrimental for a child who already suffers some mental health difficulties. I also accept the evidence that this narrative of the father as abusive will impact on the child’s ability to form, manage and have positive relationships with others. If the child is to have any chance to grow and develop into a functioning adult able to form trusting relationships, the narrative needs to change.
There is no evidence that the mother has any capacity to change this narrative. Indeed, the evidence is that when the narrative is questioned, the mother disengages from the therapy as she did with Ms M and Ms U.
The mother has engaged the child in evidence gathering for the purposes of the final hearing. The single expert described the extract in the supervision report where the supervisor reported the child saying they attended the supervised visits to ensure there was sufficient evidence as a “blatant example of intentionally involving the child for the purpose of gaining leverage” (Transcript 24 November 2025, p. 50, line 11–12). This conclusion accords with my own.
For the reasons referred to above and the evidence of the single expert, I am satisfied that the mother’s capacity to provide for the child’s psychological and emotional needs is so significantly compromised that she is unable to meet them.
That said, the father is no paragon of parenthood. The father has clearly contributed to the conflict and hostility that exists in the parental relationship. His correspondence with the mother written either by himself or sent on his instructions has been at times vitriolic. He disparaged the mother to Ms M and conceded in the witness box that things could have been done better or done another way. It should be to his great shame that he did not adopt such a view at the time of sending the correspondence.
The father has also at times acted insensitively and been unattuned to the child’s needs. His conduct in ending the child’s engagement with Ms XX and the school counsellor was thoughtless and lacked child focus. That said, I am satisfied that he generally has the capacity for reflective parenting, is not myopically focussed on excluding the mother from the child’s life and has a greater insight to the importance and significance of the child having a relationship with both parents. I am also satisfied that he has an appreciation of the difficulties facing the child if his orders were made. He did not seek to minimise or downplay the difficulties the child will face. I am satisfied by a combination of the evidence in his affidavit and answers in cross-examination that he does have a realistic plan to manage the transition into his primary care.
I am satisfied that as between the father and the mother, the father has the greater capacity to provide for the child’s needs.

s 60CC(2)(e) – the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents and other people who are significant to the child where it is safe to do so

There is no benefit to the child of a continuation of the existing regime. It has been productive of trauma and anguish for the child and must end.
Each of the parties and the ICL by the orders they seek see a benefit to the child of having some sort of relationship with the other parent axiomatically they would not seek such an order if they did not, unless of course they are disingenuous.
I am satisfied that it is to the benefit of the child to have a relationship with both of their parents and their extended family. The issue is having recognised that benefit what orders can be made to give effect to that benefit.
Whist the mother and ICL seek orders for the child to spend unsupervised time with the father, I have no confidence it would occur. The mother has provided no evidence for why unsupervised albeit limited time is in the child’s best interest when a week earlier she sought supervised time and in evidence suggested that the child’s safety required no time. It is apparent from Exhibit 81 that the mother is incapable of having the child attend on supervised visits. It would be naive to suddenly assume in those circumstances an order for unsupervised time would occur. One consequence of the orders of the mother and ICL could be applications for contravention and the perpetuation of conflict.
The mother and ICL also propose an order that the father be permitted to communicate with the child by sending gifts and letters. Assuming the gifts and letters were given to the child, the real concern is the message that would be conveyed. In that respect, I note the evidence of the single expert that the message the mother sends either overt or covertly is that the father is a dangerous person. I am not satisfied that messages or gifts as contemplated by the orders of the ICL and the mother enable the child to maintain a relationship with the father. It could quite possibly just reinforce the mother’s narrative. In that respect, I note what Ms M reported to the single expert of the ways the mother interpreted the father’s conduct where she says as follows:
...She opined that [Ms Tevye] could not entertain alternate possibilities to his behaviour, for example, if her father forgot to pack a snack, it was framed as covert and damaging behaviour, rather than an oversight. This was then interpreted as abusive behaviour by [Ms Tevye] which then [X] took on board - causing [X] distress.
It is entirely possible that the gifts and letters would be construed and presented to the child as a continuation of the mother’s narrative of abuse.
I am satisfied that placing the child in the care of the father would ensure that the child maintains a relationship with both parents. I am satisfied the father would comply with an order to ensure the child maintained a relationship with the mother; it was not suggested to him that he would not. The transition for the mother from a supervised regime to an unsupervised regime would, as opined by the single expert, very much depend upon how the mother responds and conducts herself. However, I recognise placing the child in the care of the father will mean that the child’s relationship with the mother will be significantly different including in all probability the relationship with the maternal grandmother and will be distressing to the child.

s 60CC(2)(f) – anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the case

There is no other matter not otherwise addressed that is relevant to the circumstances of this child.

DISPOSITION

In her Report, the single expert recorded the following:
At the centre of this matter is whether the parents pose a risk of harm to [X], and if so, if and how that risk may be ameliorated to allow [X] to have the benefit of a meaningful relationship with both parents. It is however noted that it is very unlikely that the parents have the capacity to coparent given the longstanding history of this matter. To promote the safety and wellbeing of [X], her parenting arrangements should minimise her exposure to parental conflict by having changeovers at school or by a third party. [X] is in a critical stage of her development as she approaches adolescence and she should be placed in the primary care of the parent who is most likely to provide emotional stability, disengage from the conflict and refrain from exposing [X] to the conflict, promote her relationship with the other parent, and support her mental health needs by working collaboratively with health professionals to prevent any further psychological harm to [X].
By the time of her cross-examination, she agreed that the parties effectively presented the Court with a binary outcome; that is, live with the mother and supervised time with the father or live with the father and supervised time with the mother. Albeit I note the mother’s orders changed by the time of submissions to move to less time but unsupervised.
I asked the single expert which of the two proposals presented the least risk for the child, which she answered as follows:
So the risk can be, I think, compartmentalised into various factors, though, because the risk in the current situation is that [X] won’t have a relationship with the father. The mother has not encouraged that relationship. That is unlikely to change. Therefore, the attitude that the mother has about the father or as – as – general about men, if I can put it that way, is that that will then be imposed on [X] as well. That will then impact on their relationships and ability to form peer relationships and intimate relationships moving forward. The risk in the other situation is a little bit different – is that in the short term, yes, [X] will experience distress. It’s unlikely that it won’t be the case. But I think, in those circumstances, she – she probably won’t – I mean, she – well, she – she won’t be able to have a relationship with her mother, and that will be – she will be very angry about that. There will be a very angry response from [X], and I think that she may hold resentment about that. Yes, that might change over time, but we do not know. That is an unknown – how [X] will respond to that change in residency. And that is always a risk in these kind of matters when there’s a change, about will the child settle or will the child deteriorate. [X] has, if I can put it, special needs because of the mental health of this child. So it requires the parent that has primary care of [X] to be able to engage with services that is going to support those mental health needs.

Well, if I look at the history---? ---Yes.

---and the – notwithstanding there has been an order for equal shared parental responsibility – that has been ignored, then the evidence is that the mother has not been able to – the mother thinks she has been dealing with it, but in reality she hasn’t? ---I agree, and I think the engagement with services has been more about evidence-gathering for – for report purposes, and in this time, [X] has not been able to get the help that they need, which is a concern that that hasn’t happened. You know, on the other hand, the father is not against therapy for the child, but he wants the child to engage appropriately with – with a service provider, and the service provider to then also engage with [X] and the family appropriately as well.

So that when I look at it, there’s the long-term risks of not having a relationship at all with her father against short-term distress? ---Yes.

Possibly long-term resentment ---?---Yes.

---against her father for making her disengage from her mother, but she at least has the opportunity of a relationship with both parents? Yes.

Does it come down to simple as that, almost? ---Yes.

(Transcript 24 November 2025 p.70 line 19 – p.71 line 12)
I accept the evidence of the single expert.
The mother’s counsel’s submissions explaining the change from supervised to unsupervised time are absent an evidential foundation and consequentially carry no weight. I am not satisfied that the change in the mother’s position represents any real change to the potential outcomes for the child. The mother sees no benefit in the relationship and has not been able to comply with supervised time. In those circumstances, it is disingenuous of her to propose unsupervised time.
The parties have no capacity to co-parent. Consistent with the single expert’s opinion, the child needs to be placed with a parent who will:
(1) provide emotional stability;

(2) disengage from the conflict;

(3) refrain from exposing the child to the conflict;

(4) promote a relationship with the other parent; and

(5) support the child’s mental health needs by working collaboratively with health professionals.

The ICL submits that the Court should make orders that the child live with the mother, that the mother have sole parental responsibility, but that orders should be made for the child to spend unsupervised time with the father between 9.00 am and 5.00 pm on the first Sunday of each month. The mother’s final position adopted many of the orders proposed by the ICL and said time should also be unsupervised but occur on only four occasions a year for three hours.
The proposals of the mother and the ICL do not meet what the single expert opines the child needs. Their respective proposals do not lead to disengagement from the conflict; indeed, they are highly likely to lead to more conflict and litigation with potential applications for contravention when, given the history of noncompliance with orders for supervised time, unsupervised time does not occur. They place the child in the primary care of a parent who has no interest in promoting a relationship with the other parent. They do not address the long-term psychological risks for the child, and they place the child with a primary carer who will only support engaging with health professionals who agree with her view of the father as the perpetrator of family violence and an abuser. The proposal is the antithesis of what is in the child’s best interest in exposing the child to an unacceptable risk of long-term harm.
The proposal of the father is one that on balance meets the requirements identified by the single expert. His proposal is more likely to lead to a disengagement from the conflict. It places the child in the primary care of a parent who is interested in promoting a relationship with the other parent. It addresses the long-term psychological risks for the child and places the child with a primary carer who will support engaging with health professionals to meet the child’s mental health needs.
I accept the evidence of the single expert that it is not too late for the child. The single expert in her oral evidence opined as follows:
And to your mind, the way – I beg your pardon – to your mind the balance of risk for [X] lies in favour of changing her residence, even though that might create a short term problems for her? --- We do need to balance those risks, don’t we, that – and I have noted that in my report that the risks are in the short term, she will experience distress in her father’s care. But then she may then settled. However, if she remains in her mother’s care, the status quo will remain. And then where does that leave her as a functional adolescent and adult moving forward with peers, with relationships, is my concern.

(Transcript 24 November 2025 p.63 lines 12–19)
I accept it will be distressing and stressful for the child and involve a significant change, but I have the confidence that the father will be able to deal with the change sensitively. Given the findings as to family violence and abuse, I accept the evidence of the single evidence that the child’s rejection of the father is reflective of a coping mechanism. It will have the benefit of removing the child from an enmeshed environment that is psychologically harmful. I am satisfied that it is the only proposal that is in the child’s best interests.
The single expert recommended that the mother’s time will need to be supervised, and I accept that recommendation. The single expert said the following:
And it would only be after a period of supervision where the mother has essentially demonstrated that she can operate in a world when [X] lives with her dad?---Yes.

That then supervision might be lifted and something far more natural - - -?---Yes.

- - - might be able to happen?---Yes.

...

So does that mean that on the father’s proposal – or, no, let’s put it another way. Does that mean that if the court determined that she should live with her father, that time with her mother needs to be supervised?---That was the father’s proposal, and I think that - - -

No, no, no, I’m asking - - -?---Sorry.

- - - you - - -?---Yes, it does

- - - does that mean - - -?---Yes.

- - - does that – is that the necessary consequence - - -?---Yes.

- - - of a change of residence – is that the mother’s time should be supervised?---Yes.

And I presume, given that [X] has lived through this for the last decade or more, that it would be supervised indefinitely?---That would be – that would be necessary.

(Transcript 24 November 2025 p.42 lines 26–31; p.69 line 40 – p.70 line 9)
Given the expert evidence is that the mother’s time will need to be supervised indefinitely, I propose to make such an order conscious as I am that such an order is not one usually made. However, the circumstances and the expert evidence demand it.
While I propose to adopt most of the father’s orders, I am not satisfied that all the orders are appropriate. A period of six months before seeing the mother does not accord with the single expert’s recommendation and is too long. The single expert proposed three months but I consider the child will miss the mother enormously so I will provide for a period of 10 weeks which enables the child to at least settle in the father’s home but is not too long before resuming time with the mother.
The father proposed that the mother’s time be limited to a minimum of two hours per fortnight on a supervised basis. The mother made no submissions about what the time should be if the Court made orders for the child to live with the father. I am not satisfied that two hours is sufficient for the child to spend with the mother and will provide a minimum of four hours. That provides an opportunity for the child to spend quality time with her mother without compromising the ability of a supervisor to ensure that no untoward statements are made about the father or that undermine the Court’s orders. In considering the duration of the visits, I have had regard to the orders the mother proposed on a final basis which limited the child’s time with the father to only three hours on a few occasions a year. I am satisfied that time with the mother should be longer and more frequent albeit supervised.
I do not propose to impose an order that if the child contacts the mother, then the moratorium period is extended as to do so would only punish the child. It may however be very relevant to any consideration in the future of any proposal moving the mother’s time from supervised to unsupervised. The father sought orders that the mother engage with a therapist. I do not propose to make that order. Whilst I am confident that it would be to the mother’s benefit to engage with an appropriately qualified objectively minded therapist, I do not intend to impose upon her such an order. In the event she does, then she is at liberty to provide to the therapist a copy of the single expert’s report and a copy of the reasons for judgment. If she does, and she gains some insight into her behaviour and its consequences then it may be the first step in a long road to ensuring she can eventually play a positive role in what remains of her child’s childhood.
I am satisfied that the above arrangements are ones that are in the best interests of the child.

COSTS

The ICL sought an order that the parties share equally the costs of the ICL. The Act provides that each party pays their own costs, subject to the provisions of s 114UB(2) which permits the Court to make such orders as to costs as it considers just if there are circumstances which so justify. No one matter set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act is determinative. Section 114UC permits the making of an order for the costs of the ICL in such proportions as the Court determines.
The father made no submissions as to costs, while the mother’s counsel suggested that the mother was exempt from paying filing fees. That does not provide her with an immunity from costs. I further note her Costs Notice makes clear she is not in receipt of legal aid.
The ICL played a significant role in the proceedings. No party sought their discharge, and I note the mother’s Initiating Application sought the appointment of an ICL. I am satisfied that these circumstances justify an order that the parties meet the costs of the ICL and meet them equally.
